title: Expressivity: AI Mental Model #6
description:
=========================================================
📓 Free visual lecture notes for this episode:
https://vizuaraai.github.io/great-mental-models-of-ai/lecture-06-expressivity.html  
=========================================================
If you cannot solve it, lift it.

When a problem refuses to yield, maybe it is not impossible. Maybe you are asking it in too few dimensions.

This is Lecture 6 of The Great Mental Models of Artificial Intelligence. In this episode, we explore Expressivity: the idea that richer, wider, higher-dimensional representations can make hidden patterns visible.

Compression squeezes things down to find the essence. Expressivity does the opposite: it lifts things up into a bigger space, where the answer has room to appear.

In this lecture, we look at:
(1) Why expressivity is the opposite of compression
(2) How XOR becomes separable when lifted into 3D
(3) The kernel trick and higher-dimensional classification
(4) Why higher-degree curves can capture richer patterns
(5) How model scale creates new capabilities in LLMs
(6) Why transformer feed-forward layers project tokens up to wider spaces
(7) How CNNs lift images into stacks of feature maps
(8) How Fourier features help NeRFs capture sharp detail
(9) Why too much expressivity can overfit noise  

#ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #Expressivity #NeuralNetworks #LLM #Transformers #ComputerVision #Vizuara

title: The Chip Trick Huawei Used to Beat ASML — Without a Single EUV Machine
description:
China was cut off from EUV lithography in 2022 — the one machine the entire chip industry agrees is mandatory. Four years later, Huawei laid out a roadmap to 1.4-nanometer chips without it. Here's the trick.

This is the story of LogicFolding and the Tau Scaling Law: two ideas He Tingbo's HiSilicon team presented at IEEE ISCAS in Shanghai that try to reach the leading edge through architecture instead of lithography. Instead of drawing a finer line, Huawei folded the logic vertically and started scaling time instead of space. We separate the proven numbers from the press release — including the heat problem nobody has solved yet — and ask what it means for ASML, TSMC, and the whole strategy of export controls.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO FROM THE CHANNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
None of this makes sense until you understand why one machine became the chokepoint the entire industry quietly depends on. That's where this war actually began:
▶ The Hidden Company Every AI Chip Secretly Depends On

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Closed Door
0:41 Why the Ban Mattered
1:31 Why Everyone Said It Was Impossible
2:43 Computational Origami
4:01 The Tau Scaling Law
4:56 Proven vs Promised
6:17 The Heat Problem Nobody Solved
6:38 Who Wins and Who Loses
7:20 The Wall Just Cracked
8:05 Where This Story Began

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- EUV lithography — extreme ultraviolet light used to carve the smallest features onto silicon; the single chokepoint China lost access to in 2022.
- LogicFolding — redesigning a single chip's blueprint so the logic is folded vertically (built up, not spread flat). Not stacking finished chips; "computational origami."
- Tau Scaling Law — Huawei's reframe: scale time, not space. Optimize how fast the signal moves rather than how small the transistor is.
- HiSilicon — Huawei's chip-design arm, kept alive through six years of sanctions.
- He Tingbo — head of HiSilicon, the engineer China calls its "chip queen," who presented these ideas.
- IEEE ISCAS 2026 — the Shanghai conference (May 25, 2026) where the roadmap was laid out.
- ASML — the only company that builds EUV scanners; "the most valuable machine on Earth."
- Kirin (end of 2026) — described at ~238 million transistors/mm², in the range of TSMC's 3nm-class processes (for context, TSMC N3E sits ~167M/mm²).
- 1.4nm-class — Huawei targets it around 2031; TSMC is expected to ship real 1.4nm around 2028.

Every spec above is Huawei's own claim, presented at ISCAS. Treat the figures as a roadmap, not a result.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
How did Huawei reach advanced chips without EUV? By changing the architecture instead of the lithography — folding the logic vertically (LogicFolding) and optimizing signal speed instead of transistor size (Tau Scaling Law), so it packs more logic into the same footprint without a finer line.

What is LogicFolding? Redesigning a single chip's blueprint from scratch so the logic is built up vertically rather than spread out flat — "computational origami." It is not gluing or stacking finished chips together.

What is the Tau Scaling Law? Huawei's framing in which "scaling" means scaling time instead of space: optimize how fast the signal travels through the transistor rather than how physically small it is.

Did Huawei actually beat ASML and TSMC? No. TSMC is expected to ship real 1.4nm around 2028; Huawei targets ~2031. The gap changed shape but did not vanish — and the heat from folding logic vertically is a problem nobody has solved yet.

Is this real or a press release? Both. About 381 chips have reportedly been mass-produced under these principles over six years, but every density and efficiency figure is Huawei's own number, presented by the company that benefits most from you believing it.

#Huawei #EUV #Semiconductors #ASML #ChipWar

title: Gemma Playground: Parallel Agents in Action
description:
Ian Ballantyne, Developer Relations Engineer at Google DeepMind, demonstrates Gemma 4's multi-agent capabilities by spinning up 10 independent subagents on a single local machine. A high-level prompt goes to an orchestrator, which delegates the work, and within seconds the agents return a complete SVG art gallery, coded locally, simultaneously, and autonomously.

What's covered: Running the Gemma 4 26B model locally, batch processing across 10 concurrent agents, orchestrator-and-subagent task delegation, inference speeds above 170 tokens per second, and what parallel local agents unlock for enterprise workflows and private on-prem deployments.

What would you build with parallel local agents? Drop it in the comments.

Subscribe to Google for Developers → https://goo.gle/developers  

Speaker: Ian Ballantyne
Products Mentioned:  Google AI, Gemini

title: He won a Nobel here for AlphaFold. Then he left. - John Jumper
description:
This episode is sponsored by Notion. Learn more about Notion's Developer Platform today at https://notion.com/mlst

Protein folding stalled biology for fifty years. A sequence of amino acids dictates a three-dimensional shape, but reading that shape meant a year and roughly $100,000 of crystallography per structure. Then AlphaFold 2 won CASP14 so decisively the organizers called the problem essentially solved.

In this documentary cut, John Jumper, who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry and has since left DeepMind for Anthropic, walks Tim Scarfe through what the system did and, more interestingly, what it did not. The architecture gets a proper dissection: MSAs, the Evoformer, invariant point attention, the FAPE loss, and Jumper's correction of the equivariance story, which ablations valued at roughly 2.5 of 30 GDT points rather than the whole win. He is blunt about the limits. AlphaFold predicts one experiment extraordinarily well; it is not a model of the cell, it does not capture dynamics, and on a given drug target it is "wrong nine times out of ten."

From there: the AlphaFold Database of 200M+ predicted structures, AlphaFold 3 and ligands, Isomorphic Labs, and Jumper's quarrel with the bitter lesson, where finite data and human hypotheses still matter. Emmanuel Nji of BioStruct Africa closes the film on what changes when work that took years now takes months, and on training the next thousand structural biologists across Africa.

---
TIMESTAMPS:
00:00:00 Cold open: predicting nature with a button press
00:01:03 The protein folding bottleneck and CASP
00:04:39 The Nobel, the database, and the move to Anthropic
00:07:39 Sponsor and framing: what AlphaFold does not claim
00:07:39 Proteins as self-assembling nanomachines
00:12:24 From structures to biology: drug discovery and Midnolin
00:17:37 The humility of AlphaFold: a narrow predictor
00:22:18 Inside the architecture: Evoformer, IPA and FAPE
00:30:20 Ruthless empiricism: ablations and 100x in data
00:35:20 Predict, control, understand
00:40:00 Against the bitter lesson; AlphaFold 3 as diffusion
00:45:07 Intelligence, representations and AGI
00:49:23 Epilogue: AlphaFold in Africa
00:52:16 Closing: the case for hybrid science models

---

title: AI Security After Codex and Claude Code — Zico Kolter & Matt Fredrikson, Gray Swan
description:
AI agents are becoming powerful enough to write code, browse the web, access private data, and act on our behalf — but the security model for this new world is still being invented. In this episode, Gray Swan cofounders Zico Kolter and Matt Fredrikson join swyx to explain why AI security is not just “cybersecurity with AI,” why agents introduce a new class of vulnerabilities, and why the next major AI incident may be a gray swan: unlikely, but clearly visible before it happens.

We go deep on prompt injection, automated red teaming, model robustness, agent identity, computer-use agents, enterprise guardrails, and the emerging AI insurance/compliance stack. Zico and Matt also explain why frontier models are not automatically safer as they scale, why specialized red-teaming models can now beat humans at breaking AI systems, and why the future of AI security may depend on AI systems attacking, defending, and interpreting other AI systems.

We discuss:
• Why AI systems need a different security mindset from traditional software
• How prompt injection creates a new exploit class for agents like Codex and Claude Code
• Gray Swan Arena and the rise of community red teaming
• Shade: AI that can outperform humans at breaking models
• Why LLMs are an alien form of intelligence that fail differently from humans
• Human vs browser-agent robustness and why humans ranked fourth
• Why eval awareness and capability elicitation matter
• Cygnal: Gray Swan’s guardrail model for policy enforcement
• Why bigger models do not automatically become more robust
• The lethal trifecta: untrusted data, private data, and exfiltration
• Why “just prompt it better” is not enough for enterprise AI security
• OpenClaw, computer-use agents, and the agent security nightmare
• Agent-native identity, permissions, and enterprise deployment
• Why AI security may become part of insurance and compliance
• Why the first major AI prompt-injection breach may be inevitable

—

Gray Swan
• Website: https://www.grayswan.ai/

Zico Kolter
• X: https://x.com/zicokolter
• Website: https://zicokolter.com/
• LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/zico-kolter-560382a4/

title: 🎥 Statistics 4.5 – Combining Probability & Counting Techniques | “At Least,” “At Most,” & More
description:
Welcome to Chapter 4.5: Combining Probability and Counting Techniques! In this lesson, we bring together everything you’ve learned about probability, combinations, permutations, and counting principles — and apply them to more complex, real‑world problems. These are the types of questions where students often get stuck, and this chapter shows you exactly how to break them down.

We’ll cover:
✅ How to recognize key probability phrases and translate them into math
— “At least n”  
— “At most n”   
— “Greater than n”
— “Less than n” 
— “Between n and m”   
— Why these phrases determine the counting method you use
✅ How to decide whether to use:
— Combinations (order doesn’t matter)
— Permutations (order matters)
— Addition Rule for “or”
— Multiplication Rule for “and”
— Complement Rule for “at least” problems
✅ How to combine counting techniques with probability formulas
— Using 𝑛𝐶𝑟 and 𝑛𝑃𝑟
 inside probability calculations
— When to add vs. when to multiply
— How to avoid double‑counting
✅ Step‑by‑step examples:
— Forming groups of at least 10 tourists
— Multi‑stage selection problems
— Using combinations with the Fundamental Counting Principle
— Real‑world scenarios that require both counting and probability
✅ Spreadsheet setup for evaluating combinations, permutations, and probabilities efficiently

You’ll see how to decode word problems, choose the correct method, and combine counting techniques with probability rules to solve more advanced questions. By the end of the video, you’ll be able to handle “at least,” “at most,” and multi‑stage probability problems with confidence.

🎓 Perfect for:  
Introductory Statistics students
Business, social science, health, or STEM majors
Anyone learning how to apply counting methods to real‑world probability problems

title: The Breakthrough That Could End Silicon Forever — Built by imec, ASML and TSMC
description:
The transistor can't shrink anymore. At roughly 50 silicon atoms wide, electrons tunnel straight through a closed switch — and that physical wall is where Moore's Law quietly ends. This is the first post-silicon chip to leave the lab.

In June 2026, a team at imec — working with ASML and TSMC — integrated complementary 2D transistors (molybdenum disulfide for n-type, a tungsten-based partner for p-type, each a single atom thick) on a standard 300mm wafer at 50nm contacted poly pitch, with 94% of them operational. That number is the story: 94% is the line between a physics experiment and something a factory can actually build. It's the first time two-dimensional logic crossed from the lab to the fab.

This video breaks down why silicon hit a wall (quantum tunneling), why one-atom-thick 2D materials barely leak, who wins and loses in the race (Nvidia, TSMC, Intel), and the crucial distinction almost everyone blurs: a NEW MATERIAL (2D semiconductors) versus a NEW ARCHITECTURE (Huawei's LogicFolding 3D stacking, forced by ASML export sanctions). And the honest part: this is a milestone, not a finish line — years, not months.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO FROM THE CHANNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
None of this happens without one company most people have never heard of — the one that prints every switch, no matter what it's made of. Watch the prequel:
▸ "The Hidden Company Every AI Chip Secretly Depends On" (the ASML / Carl Zeiss story — not Nvidia)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The switch that stopped switching
0:20 Moore's Law was a habit, not a law
1:01 The wall: quantum tunneling
1:32 Why the leak means heat and cost
2:45 A semiconductor one atom thick
3:47 The lab-to-fab moment: imec, ASML, TSMC
4:57 Who wins, who loses
5:41 Huawei's different bet: stacking, not replacing
7:24 The chip you'll hold in 2032
8:13 The company underneath it all

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Quantum tunneling — electrons leaking through a gate thinner than ~10nm
- Moore's Law — the doubling "promise," not a law of physics
- 2D materials — semiconductors a single atom thick (~0.65nm)
- Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) — the n-type channel
- Tungsten disulfide / diselenide (WS₂ / WSe₂) — the p-type partner
- Complementary logic — n-type + p-type on the same wafer
- 300mm wafer · 50nm pitch · 94% operational — the lab-to-fab metrics
- imec, ASML, TSMC — the three companies in the room
- Huawei LogicFolding — 3D stacking (new architecture, not new material)
- Lab-to-fab — moving from a demo to factory-scale manufacturing

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Is silicon dead? No. This is a milestone, not a finish line — expect years of yield, contamination and integration work before post-silicon chips ship. Anyone saying silicon dies next year is selling something.

What is a 2D semiconductor? A material a single atom thick (like molybdenum disulfide, ~0.65nm) used to build transistors that barely leak current, because the channel is too thin for the quantum tunneling that kills shrinking silicon.

Why can't silicon transistors keep shrinking? Below ~10nm of gate length, electrons tunnel through the closed gate, so "off" stops meaning off. That wastes energy as heat and breaks the clean 1/0 a switch depends on.

What's the difference between 2D materials and Huawei's LogicFolding? 2D materials replace the silicon switch with a new material. LogicFolding keeps silicon but stacks it in 3D — a new architecture working around the wall, not removing it. Huawei took that path because ASML export sanctions cut it off from the most advanced lithography.

Who made the first post-silicon chip? A team at imec, working with ASML and TSMC, demonstrated it at a major semiconductor symposium in 2026 on a 300mm wafer with 94% of transistors operational.

Which company does every chip secretly depend on? Not Nvidia, not even TSMC — the lithography maker that prints every switch. That's the subject of the related video above.

#semiconductors #MooresLaw #2Dmaterials #ASML #postsilicon

title: GLM-5.2 vs Claude Opus 4.8: Can Open Weights Match a Closed Frontier Model?
description:
GLM 5.2 is open-weights and roughly 6x cheaper than Opus 4.8 — so I ran them head-to-head on three identical tasks to see if that price gap shows up in the output.

Sign up for my FREE weekly newsletter, where I spill my unfiltered thoughts on the latest AI news, cool research, and projects I'm building: https://www.onchainaigarage.com/

🐦 Follow Tonbi on X for real-time AI x blockchain updates! https://x.com/tonbistudio

A fair head-to-head between GLM 5.2 (Z.ai's 753B open-weights, MIT-licensed model) and Opus 4.8 (the most capable closed model you can use right now), both run on the exact same prompts. The big setup is the price gap — GLM 5.2 is around 4-6x cheaper across input and output, on top of being open weights you could run yourself. I give each model three identical challenges: a 3D Three.js fantasy world built from scratch, an instruction-following "torture test" admin console with 40 exact rules, and a YouTube production assistant that turns notes into a full launch package. To keep it honest, I hand the instruction-heavy builds to Codex to score them objectively, then tally it all up at the end. The real question: can a model at a fraction of the cost actually keep up with the frontier?

Timestamps:
0:00 - The matchup: GLM 5.2 vs Opus 4.8
0:54 - The contenders + the price gap that matters
2:58 - Task 1: a 3D fantasy world from scratch
8:38 - Task 2: the instruction-following torture test
10:56 - Judging task 2 with Codex
13:03 - Task 3: a YouTube production assistant
18:03 - Judging task 3 with Codex
20:34 - The final tally: can 1/6 the price keep up?

Have you tried GLM 5.2 against the frontier models yet? Tell me what you'd have tested in the comments. If you want more head-to-heads like this, please like, subscribe, and hit the bell! 🦐✨

title: Self-Hosted VDI Made Easy: Kasm and Proxmox Autoscaling
description:

title: GPT 5.5 Is Smarter Than Me — But I Have More Context
description:
GPT 5.5 is already smarter than most of us. So what makes you irreplaceable as an engineer? According to OpenAI's Romain Huet, it's context — and Codex is how you give the model everything it needs to become a true teammate.

Filmed live at a Tessl HQ in London, this is an unscripted, demo-heavy 17-minute talk covering what's new in Codex: 120+ plugins, computer use that runs in the background without stealing your machine, an in-app browser for commenting directly on your UI, AI-generated game assets, Codex Spark running on Cerebras chips, and a live audience-driven game build from scratch.

What we cover:
– Why context — not raw intelligence — is what makes an engineer valuable alongside AI
– Computer use that gives agents their own cursor while you keep using your computer
– The in-app browser: comment on your UI to direct the agent in real time
– Generating game assets and building playable games with image gen and Codex
– Codex Spark: the faster model running on Cerebras chips

title: Make Legal Write Your Evals: Building Jade, Chime’s Financial Copilot | Interrupt 26
description:
Philipp Comans, software engineer at Chime, explains how his team built Jade — Chime's always-on financial co-pilot — and solved one of the hardest problems in production AI: getting non-engineers to meaningfully participate in evals. By structuring compliance risk as a taxonomy, using Giskard to generate adversarial test cases, and closing the loop with LangSmith, Chime turned their legal and compliance partners into active co-authors of their eval pipeline. The result: faster releases, fewer surprises at the release gate, and compliance trust built continuously rather than at the end.

Chapters:
0:00 Intro and what Chime's Jade actually does
1:04 Why "oops-driven development" breaks trust (and invites regulators)
1:51 The core question: how do you know your agent is compliant?
2:09 The problem with the traditional compliance model
2:52 What the better model looks like
3:13 Evals as your alignment surface
3:55 The language barrier between engineers and legal
4:19 Five steps to bridge the gap
4:36 What evals actually are: LLM-as-a-judge, explained simply
5:32 Step 1: Creating structure with a risk taxonomy
6:55 Handing the taxonomy back to legal to define in their own words
7:52 From risk definition to dataset: how Giskard generates adversarial questions
9:25 "I can't give you investment advice, but Nvidia has been on a tear"
9:48 Step 2: Building the LLM-as-a-judge evaluator from the same risk doc
10:35 Running evals in LangSmith and reading the results
11:06 Making scores visible at every level: engineers, compliance, execs
11:38 Step 3: The feedback flywheel — four ways one annotation improves the system
13:07 What this bought Chime: velocity, alignment, and trust
13:44 Five takeaways

title: Ooverlay vs component architecture? here's what breaks
description:
You shipped an AI feature this quarter. What you actually shipped was a non-deterministic component into the middle of a deterministic flow — and it's quietly breaking your state machines, your cache, and your tests.

This is the architectural diagnosis behind the AI feature overload of this year. The problem was never "AI writes bad code." It's that wrapping an unpredictable LLM into a system that depended on prediction changes the physics of that system: the same input can now return three different answers, caching becomes a guess, tests soften from assertEquals to assertProbablyOkay, and you lose the ability to hold your own architecture in your head. We walk through why non-determinism breaks the invariant a state machine relies on, what the death of mechanical sympathy costs you in real milliseconds (340µs compiled vs 480ms+ through an external model), the feature-parity trap that pressures good teams into shipping it anyway, and the "ghost code" of unowned prompts running your most load-bearing logic. The fix is a shift from AI-first to architecture-first: treat the model as a bounded component, not an overlay.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VIDEO RELACIONADO DEL CANAL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This is the diagnosis of the collapse. The cause is the debt it was bolted onto:
▶ The AI Code Debt Bomb is About to Explode

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CAPÍTULOS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 Cold open: the anomaly
0:57 The surface-level illusion
2:13 When the same input returns three answers
2:58 Non-determinism breaks the state machine
4:24 The death of mechanical sympathy
5:40 The feature-parity trap
6:51 Ghost code and borrowed heartbeats
8:11 The architecture-first fix

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTOS MENCIONADOS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Non-determinism — the same input no longer guarantees the same output once an LLM sits in the flow.
- Determinism / the invariant — the guarantee a state machine relies on: state A + input X always goes to state B.
- State machine — predictable, cacheable, testable transition graph; broken when a model decides a transition.
- Mechanical sympathy — writing software that respects how the machine underneath actually works.
- LLM wrapper — a thin layer over an external API that hides where the intelligence actually runs.
- The feature-parity trap — structural deadline pressure that ships brittle integrations even when teams know better.
- Ghost code — load-bearing prompts with no real author and no spec, that you can only poke and hope.
- Break-even / the reliability loan — the crossover where keeping a brittle abstraction alive costs more than the feature earned.
- Architecture-first — treat the model as a bounded component with a hard boundary, timeout, and deterministic fallback.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREGUNTAS FRECUENTES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
What is non-deterministic AI in software architecture? It's when an LLM in your flow can return different outputs for the same input, removing the predictability deterministic systems are built on.

Why do LLM wrappers break state machines? A state machine depends on an invariant — the same input always produces the same transition. A model can decide differently on Tuesday than Monday, so the invariant, and the cache and tests built on it, collapse.

What is mechanical sympathy? Writing software that respects how the underlying machine actually works — cooperating with a fast, predictable core instead of bridging it to something slow and unpredictable in the hot path.

What does architecture-first mean for AI features? Treating the model as a bounded component, not a system-wide overlay: give it a hard boundary, validate its output before it re-enters a deterministic flow, and add a timeout with a deterministic fallback.

Is the problem that AI writes bad code? No. The mistake is architectural, not moral — it's about non-determinism contaminating deterministic flows and who owns the spec, not the quality of generated code.

#SoftwareArchitecture #TechnicalDebt #AIEngineering #SoftwareEngineering #SystemDesign

title: 0.1% Tsinghua AI Researcher Gives Paper Ideas For Robot RL
description:
Next Sunday Skool LIVE: Build LLM In 1 Prompt - Get live 1-on-1 help in a weekly group call - write papers, train LLMs: https://www.skool.com/become-ai-researcher-2669/about

arxiv - https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08508

📆 Schedule 1 on 1 with me - https://cal.com/vuk-ai/60-min

A Tsinghua robotics researcher (PhD in Hong Kong) walks through a paper that combines sampling-based MPC with a reinforcement learning controller for robot manipulation. The idea: keep RL for low-level control, then stack a sampling-based planner on top to actually complete high-level tasks like moving a desk or making coffee. He also argues the architecture shouldn't be static — you can distill the sampling planner back into a policy, so RL and sampling blend into one "fluid" system.

0:00 A Tsinghua researcher on robotics RL
0:31 RL is great at low-level control, not whole tasks
1:04 Put a sampling-based MPC planner on top
1:40 RL vs classical planning: which part goes where
2:27 Distill sampling into a policy (a fluid architecture)

title: The Psychology of Liminal Spaces
description:
What makes a liminal space so deeply unsettling — and why can't we look away?

Subscribe to StudioBinder Academy ►► https://bit.ly/sb-ad
StudioBinder Blog ►► http://bit.ly/sb-bl

─────────────────────

Chapters:
00:00 - Backrooms and Liminality Introduction
00:51 - Liminality Defined
01:52 - History of Liminality
03:51 - Qualities of Liminal Spaces
07:42 - Why Liminal Spaces Now?
09:24 - Takeaways

─────────────────────

Backrooms is a box-office phenomenon. But to understand its power, you need to understand liminal spaces: where the concept comes from, the psychology behind it, and how filmmakers have been using it for decades.

In this video, we break down the full history of liminality — from its origins in anthropology and Jungian philosophy, to iconic uses in The Shining, Twin Peaks: The Return, and Vivarium — and explain exactly how artists manufacture that eerie feeling of "betweenness."

🎬 WHAT YOU'LL LEARN:
• What a liminal space actually is (and where the term comes from)
• The 6 psychological factors that make spaces feel liminal
• How Stanley Kubrick, David Lynch, and others used liminal spaces in their films
• Why Backrooms works — and why the concept feels so universal right now
• How to create liminal spaces in your own films

title: 155 Contributors Later: Where Kafka Is Headed ft. Andrew Schofield | Ep. 34 | Confluent Developer
description:
Listen: https://confluent.buzzsprout.com | Tim Berglund talks to Andrew Schofield (Confluent) about his career in Apache Kafka. Andrew’s first job: working on queuing systems in 1991. His challenge: working at Confluent and in the Kafka community to bring queue semantics into Kafka while also helping shape major efforts like diskless Kafka, disaster recovery, and the project’s broader evolution.

► Queues for Kafka Explained (KIP-932): https://youtu.be/Wb0xyqgaIqw

🎧 Subscribe to Confluent Developer wherever you listen to podcasts: https://confluent.buzzsprout.com
▶️ Subscribe on YouTube, and hit the 🔔 to catch new episodes: https://youtube.com/@ConfluentDeveloper?sub_confirmation=1
👍 If you enjoyed this, please like it and leave us a comment
🎧 Confluent also has a podcast for tech leaders: "Life Is But A Stream" hosted by our friend, Joseph Morais: https://www.confluent.io/life-is-but-a-stream-show

CREDITS
Hosted by Tim Berglund, Adi Polak and Viktor Gamov
Produced and Edited by Noelle Gallagher, Peter Furia and Nurie Mohamed
Music by Coastal Kites
Artwork by Phil Vo

ABOUT THE PODCAST 
Hi, we’re Tim Berglund, Adi Polak, and Viktor Gamov and we’re excited to bring you the Confluent Developer podcast (formerly “Streaming Audio.”) Our hand-crafted weekly episodes feature in-depth interviews with our community of software developers (actual human beings - not AI) talking about some of the most interesting challenges they’ve faced in their careers. We aim to explore the conditions that gave rise to each person’s technical hurdles, as well as how their experiences transformed their understanding and approach to building systems. 

Whether you’re a seasoned open source data streaming engineer, or just someone who’s interested in learning more about Apache Kafka®,  Apache Flink® and real-time data, we hope you’ll appreciate the stories, the discussion, and our effort to bring you a high-quality show worth your time.

ABOUT CONFLUENT DEVELOPER
Confluent Developer provides comprehensive resources for developers looking to learn about Apache Kafka®, Apache Flink®, Confluent Cloud, Confluent Platform, and any other technology related to the broader Data Streaming Platform. Content on Confluent Developer includes courses, getting started guides, topical deep-dives, patterns, tutorials, and listings of community events. Learn more at https://developer.confluent.io.

title: How eBPF Empowers Developers to Observe Inside the Linux Kernel in a Safe and Unintrusive Way
description:
Dan Fineran explores how eBPF has evolved far beyond its roots in packet filtering into a robust, safe way to extend the Linux kernel. He explains how the eBPF "verifier", the security guardrail, enables implementation of deep observability and networking without the risks of traditional kernel modules or the slow upstreaming process. He touches on tools like Tetragon that leverage eBPF for "front-foot" security enforcement, proactively intercepting threats such as buffer overflows before they execute, while providing visibility into file systems and drivers without intrusive instrumentation.

Read a transcript of this interview: https://bit.ly/4ew9ONB

title: Lamis Mukta - Learning while you sleep: Beyond memory to dreaming - AI Native DevCon June 2026
description: Early implementations of memory were key to managing context and paving the way toward continual learning. We'll walk through the bottlenecks we're seeing with this approach today, and introduce the concept of dreaming: a second derivative process which periodically prunes and curates memory and leads to measurably better agent performance over time. This will include an architectural overview of designing a dreaming service in practice.

title: Video 1: AI in Digital Pathology - Why Now?
description:
Digital pathology is changing fast. In this first video of the series, I explain why AI and specifically foundation models have fundamentally changed what is possible in computational pathology, and why now is the right time to learn these tools.

We cover:
- What whole slide images are and why they are challenging to work with
- The shift from task-specific models to foundation models trained on millions of pathology patches
- Why embeddings are the core concept you need to understand
- A roadmap of the full 7-video series: from patch embeddings to survival prediction

No code in this video. Pure concepts and motivation. If you are a pathologist, biologist, or ML practitioner wondering where to start with AI in pathology, this is it.

Code for this series is available at: https://github.com/bnsreenu/AI-for-Digital-Pathology

title: CSES Speedrun - Graphs - Part VI
description:
This is part 6 of me doing problems from the CSES problem set dealing with graphs. Here we get into the heavier graph theory territory: Strongly Connected Components via Kosaraju's algorithm (forward DFS for topological order + reverse-graph DFS for component assignment), 2-SAT reduced to SCC on an implication graph, SCC condensation into a DAG for max-coin pathfinding, and Eulerian circuits via Hierholzer's algorithm.

Problem Set:
https://cses.fi/problemset/

51:36 Mail Delivery (Eulerian circuit via Hierholzer's algorithm)
1:07:33 Wrap-up

title: The Tiny Idea That Lets Anyone Fine-Tune AI
description:
How can tiny trainable matrices adapt huge AI models?

In this video, we explain LoRA, or Low-Rank Adaptation: the key idea behind fine-tuning large pretrained models without updating every weight.  

We cover how LoRA freezes the base model, learns a low-rank update using two smaller matrices A and B, why initialization matters, and how LoRA can be interpreted as a compact key-value memory for task-specific adaptation.

Then we look at several important extensions:
- LoRA+: using different learning rates for A and B
- QLoRA: quantizing the frozen backbone with NF4, blockwise quantization, and double quantization
- VeRA: sharing frozen random basis matrices and learning only scaling vectors
- DoRA: separating magnitude and direction updates for stronger adaptation

By the end, you’ll understand why parameter-efficient fine-tuning works, how LoRA makes adaptation practical, and how newer variants push the idea even further.

00:00 Why Fine-Tuning Huge Models Is Expensive
01:32 LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation
06:34 LoRA Initialization
09:34 LoRA+: Different Learning Rates for A and B
10:21 QLoRA: Quantizing the Frozen Backbone
16:23 VeRA: Fewer Trainable Adapter Parameters
18:26 DoRA: Separating Magnitude and Direction
21:23 Outro

title: LLM Interview Series #5: What Is PagedAttention?
description:
==========================================================
Preparing for AI, ML, or LLM infrastructure interviews? Practice real interview-style questions here:
https://interview.vizuara.ai/
==========================================================

"What is PagedAttention?" is a deep LLM inference interview question because it tests whether you understand not just attention, but how attention is served efficiently on real GPUs.

Most candidates know that KV cache is important.

But they often do not understand the memory management problem behind it: how KV cache grows during generation, why traditional allocation wastes GPU memory, and why paging becomes so powerful for high-throughput inference.

In this video, we derive the idea on the blackboard step by step:

- GPU memory management for LLM inference
- Traditional KV cache management
- Why KV cache memory gets wasted or fragmented
- How PagedAttention works
- The step-by-step mechanism of pages and block tables
- Why this matters for serving many requests efficiently
- A brief preview of online softmax, which we will cover in the next video

A strong interview answer should not just define PagedAttention. It should explain the problem, motivate the design, walk through the mechanism, and connect it back to real inference systems.

The goal is to answer with depth, clarity, and passion, so the interviewer can see that you understand the system beyond the surface level.

==========================================================
Preparing for AI, ML, or LLM infrastructure interviews? Practice real interview-style questions here:
https://interview.vizuara.ai/
==========================================================

title: NVIDIA's Dominance Is Already Over?
description:
Last week something happened that does not add up. Amazon already has the one thing everyone says makes Nvidia untouchable, a real custom AI chip called Trainium, running serious workloads at massive scale. Amazon's own CEO said that if they sold it like a normal chip company, it would be a fifty billion dollar business. They are sold out, there is a waiting list, and the answer is still no. So if a chip this good from a company this big still can't lay a finger on Nvidia, the obvious question gets a lot more interesting. Can anyone actually beat Nvidia?

To answer it you have to drop one assumption: that this is a fight about chips. It never was. In this video we take the moat apart piece by piece and show why the chip was never really the product.

Here is what we get into:
What a chip actually is, from switches to cores to a full GPU
Why Nvidia's silicon is special: matrix multiply, tensor cores, and feeding the math
Why the most efficient chips (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium) still haven't won
Why nobody just copies CUDA, even the tools built to escape it compile back down to it
Why every cloud giant builds its own chip but not one of them will sell it to you
The one thin spot in the whole wall: inference versus training

📚 Related Resources:
→ System Design Course: https://academy.bytemonk.io/courses
→ ByteMonk Blog: https://blog.bytemonk.io/
→ LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/bytemonk/
→ Github: https://github.com/bytemonk-academy

🕐 CHAPTERS
0:00 The Amazon paradox that doesn't add up
1:14 What a chip actually is
2:00 Why Nvidia's switches are special
2:48 General vs efficient: ASICs and TPUs
3:44 So why hasn't the most efficient chip won?
4:05 Moat 1: CUDA and 15 years of software
5:30 Why nobody just copies CUDA
6:42 It's not a chip problem, it's switching cost
7:22 Moat 2: it's a system, not a chip
9:03 Moat 3: the supply wall (TSMC)
10:22 Back to Amazon: why won't they sell?
11:13 Every cloud giant builds its own chip
11:58 The real answer: three stacked moats
12:17 Why this won't last forever

title: Compression: AI Mental Model #5
description:
============================================================
📓 Free visual lecture notes for this episode:
https://vizuaraai.github.io/great-mental-models-of-ai/lecture-05-compression.html  
============================================================

When something in AI becomes too big, the answer is often not to make the model brute-force its way through it. The answer is to find the smaller space hiding inside it.

This is Lecture 5 of The Great Mental Models of Artificial Intelligence. In this episode, we explore Compression: the idea that real-world data, images, matrices, memory caches, and even model updates often contain far fewer true degrees of freedom than they appear to.

The move is simple: squeeze something large into a compact latent space, work there, and project it back up. The surprising part is how little information is lost.

============================================================
In this lecture, we look at:

(1) The narrow-waist idea behind compression
(2) Why real-world data is lower-dimensional than it looks
(3) Byte-pair encoding and shorter codes for text
(4) Stable Diffusion and why image generation happens in latent space
(5) DeepSeek MLA and compressing the KV cache
(6) LoRA and low-rank adaptation
(7) World Models and agents learning inside compressed dreams
(8) Why many AI breakthroughs come from finding the smaller hidden space
============================================================

#ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #Compression #LatentSpace #StableDiffusion #LoRA #DeepSeek #Vizuara

title: Expressivity: AI Mental Model #6
description:
=========================================================
📓 Free visual lecture notes for this episode:
https://vizuaraai.github.io/great-mental-models-of-ai/lecture-06-expressivity.html  
=========================================================
If you cannot solve it, lift it.

When a problem refuses to yield, maybe it is not impossible. Maybe you are asking it in too few dimensions.

This is Lecture 6 of The Great Mental Models of Artificial Intelligence. In this episode, we explore Expressivity: the idea that richer, wider, higher-dimensional representations can make hidden patterns visible.

Compression squeezes things down to find the essence. Expressivity does the opposite: it lifts things up into a bigger space, where the answer has room to appear.

In this lecture, we look at:
(1) Why expressivity is the opposite of compression
(2) How XOR becomes separable when lifted into 3D
(3) The kernel trick and higher-dimensional classification
(4) Why higher-degree curves can capture richer patterns
(5) How model scale creates new capabilities in LLMs
(6) Why transformer feed-forward layers project tokens up to wider spaces
(7) How CNNs lift images into stacks of feature maps
(8) How Fourier features help NeRFs capture sharp detail
(9) Why too much expressivity can overfit noise  

#ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #Expressivity #NeuralNetworks #LLM #Transformers #ComputerVision #Vizuara

title: How exactly do computers understand code? Let’s invent a programming language to find out
description:
Before code can run, a computer has to somehow turn human-readable text into instructions it can execute.

So in this video, we do exactly that from scratch. We invent a tiny programming language, write a compiler for it, and build a virtual machine that can actually run the programs we write.

You do not need to be an expert to follow along. The goal of this video is to make programming languages feel less mysterious by building one piece by piece, slowly and visually. If you have never written code before, you can watch this as a guided tour of how computers understand instructions. If you are technical, you will get a practical walkthrough of lexing, parsing, abstract syntax trees, bytecode, stack machines, function calls, local variables, and control flow.

A full table of contents is below, but at a high level we cover
1. What programming languages really are
2. How source code becomes tokens
3. How parsers turn text into structure
4. How math expressions, variables, and conditionals work
5. How functions are represented and executed
6. How compilers generate lower-level instructions
7. How a stack-based virtual machine runs code
8. Why computers do not “understand” code the way humans do

All the code written in this video: https://github.com/milen-patel/tinylang

Chapters:
00:00:00 - Introduction
00:06:20 - Basic Addition/Subtraction and Formalizing Our Intuition 
00:11:30 - Viewing Expressions as Trees

00:26:20 - Throwing Multiplication/Division into the mix (remember PEMDAS?)

05:29:20 - Making Recursion a Bit More Visible

08:54:40 - Start Implementing Functions

title: The Hidden Company Every AI Chip Secretly Depends On — And It's Not Nvidia
description:
Everyone watches Nvidia — but the real bottleneck of the AI boom is two companies almost nobody can name: ASML and Carl Zeiss SMT.

Every advanced AI chip on Earth has to pass through a single $380 million EUV lithography machine built by ASML in the Netherlands — and that machine cannot focus light without a mirror made by Zeiss in Germany, the flattest object humans have ever manufactured. This is the hidden chip supply chain that decides whether AI can keep scaling at all: Nvidia → TSMC → ASML → Zeiss. No competitor. No plan B. No second supplier.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

In this breakdown: why EUV lithography is one of the hardest engineering problems ever solved, why a machine ships in 40 containers + 3 cargo planes + 20 trucks, why China's billions still can't reproduce it, why ASML quietly paid over a billion euros for nearly a quarter of Zeiss SMT, and why Chinese headhunters (reportedly including Huawei) started poaching Zeiss and ASML engineers at three times their salary.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED — NEXT ON MACRO LENS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This entire chain of dependencies was built for silicon. But there's a material just a single atom thick that some researchers believe could make silicon transistors obsolete — and with them, every chokepoint in this video. The next Macro Lens episode, "The Material That Could Make Silicon Obsolete" (2D transistors / post-silicon), is coming soon. If that happens, the most powerful companies in tech might be guarding a door that no longer leads anywhere.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The company that holds AI's off-switch
0:39 One chokepoint, no plan B
1:40 ASML and the EUV machine
2:20 $380 million, 100,000 parts
2:49 Why nobody can copy it
3:54 The hidden layer: Carl Zeiss mirrors
4:29 The flattest object ever made
5:01 The real hidden company
5:43 The full chain: Nvidia to Zeiss
6:09 China is poaching the engineers
7:25 Who actually wins the AI boom
7:49 AI isn't software, it's physics
8:30 What if silicon is next

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- ASML — the only maker of EUV lithography systems
- EUV lithography (extreme ultraviolet) — printing circuits measured in atoms
- High-NA EUV — newest generation, ~$380M per machine
- Carl Zeiss SMT — maker of the EUV mirrors; ASML owns ~24.9% (€1B+ stake)
- TSMC — foundry that prints Nvidia's advanced chips using ASML machines
- Nvidia — designs the chips, but doesn't control whether they can exist
- Export controls — how the West froze China's advanced chip ambitions
- Huawei / China's EUV prototype — the workarounds and the poaching

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
What company does every AI chip depend on? Every advanced AI chip passes through ASML, the only maker of EUV lithography machines, which in turn depends on EUV mirrors made by Carl Zeiss SMT in Germany.

Is ASML more important than Nvidia for AI? For the supply chain, yes — Nvidia designs chips, but without ASML's EUV machine (and Zeiss's optics) those chips cannot be manufactured at all.

How much does an EUV lithography machine cost? A standard EUV machine costs around $200 million; the newest High-NA generation runs over $350 million, closer to $380 million each.

Why can't China just build its own EUV machine? China has a prototype that generates EUV light as of 2025, but printing chips at volume is a different problem separated by years of accumulated, distributed engineering know-how across thousands of suppliers.

Who makes the mirrors inside ASML's EUV machines? Carl Zeiss SMT in Oberkochen, Germany — the mirrors are so flat that scaled to the size of Germany, the largest bump would be about one millimeter.

title: Test-Time Training Explained: LLMs That Learn on the Fly
description:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.23675

What if an LLM could learn while it reads?

Most language models only use their frozen weights at inference time. They read your prompt, generate an answer, and then forget everything once the context is gone. But a new direction in AI research explores something more dynamic: models that can update temporary internal memory while processing text.

In this video, we visually explain test-time training, fast weights, and the idea of an LLM that learns at test time. Instead of only retrieving information from context, the model can adapt as it reads, forming temporary updates that help it reason over long documents, changing patterns, and new information.

We’ll break down the core intuition behind “learning while reading,” why it matters for long-context AI, how it differs from normal training and fine-tuning, and why fast weights could become an important part of future language models.

Topics covered:

What it means for an LLM to learn while reading
Test-time training explained visually
Fast weights and temporary memory
Why normal LLM inference is limited
How adaptive models could improve long-context reasoning
The difference between frozen weights, context, and learned updates

If you’re interested in LLMs, transformers, long-context AI, inference-time compute, and the future of adaptive language models, this video is for you.

title: Extreme CoDesign cuts Token Costs!
description:
VIDEO TITLE
What is Nvidia Extreme CoDesign and does it cut Token Costs?

VIDEO DESCRIPTION
This video explains NVIDIA's concept of "Extreme Co-Design," a term frequently used by CEO Jensen Huang. Extreme Co-Design refers to the practice of designing silicon, racks, networking, and software together as one integrated stack rather than treating them as separate layers. The video breaks this concept down into three key pillars: Silicon and Multi-Chip Architecture, where GPUs, DPUs, and multi-chip modules are specifically designed for large-scale parallel workloads with optimized tensor cores, high-bandwidth memory, and chip-to-chip interconnects; Rack-Scale Systems and Networking, where full rack systems like DGX and HGX are co-designed with NVLink, NVSwitch, InfiniBand, and Ethernet as a single high-bandwidth, low-latency fabric; and Software and Algorithmic Integration, where CUDA, TensorRT, Triton, and other frameworks are built to fully leverage every available hardware feature.

The video explains how these three pillars are optimized end-to-end as one unified stack, guided by profiling data from real hyper-scale AI workloads. This approach allows NVIDIA to adjust chip layouts, network topologies, kernel implementations, and model architectures together to remove bottlenecks anywhere in the system. The result is that the entire AI system behaves like a single, finely tuned engine rather than a collection of mismatched parts, ultimately reducing the cost per token by maximizing AI throughput per watt and per dollar.

The presenter invites viewers to share their thoughts in the comments on whether NVIDIA's optimized end-to-end stack will become more dominant in the industry. Additional videos on related topics are available in the playlist linked in the description, and viewers are encouraged to subscribe to support the channel's growth. The video closes with a reminder to balance technology enthusiasm with outdoor activities and real-world experiences.

title: Why LLMs Use Weird Numbers: FP8, BF16, INT4
description:
If you are reading the description, you found the hidden exponent.

Most people skip this part, so here is your technical treat:

One reason BF16 became so popular in AI is that it keeps the same exponent size as Float32. Float32 uses 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 fraction bits. BF16 uses 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and only 7 fraction bits.

That means BF16 has much less precision than FP32, but it can still represent roughly the same range of very small and very large values. For neural networks, that tradeoff is often more useful than it sounds, because training can be very sensitive to values becoming too large or too small. So BF16 is not simply “half of Float32.”

It is more like:

“Keep the range. Sacrifice the detail.”

That design choice is one reason it became so important for modern AI training.

title: Why LLMs Waste 99% of Compute — And How KV Cache Fixes It
description:
Your AI model secretly redoes the SAME math millions of times — every single time it replies to you.

Ever wonder why ChatGPT slows down in long chats? The answer lives inside Transformer attention, and it's called the KV cache.
00:00 — Intro
00:44 — Attention during Training
02:03 — Inference (Generation Time)
03:40 — Problem with Attention
07:45 — Solution: KV Cache
09:40 — Memory issue


In this deep dive into LLM inference, you'll learn:

✅ Why attention recomputes Keys & Values for every token — the hidden bottleneck behind every chatbot reply
✅ How the KV cache turns quadratic attention cost into linear cost (real numbers: 32 million → just 8,000 projections)
✅ Why caching trades compute for VRAM, and how a 100K-token chat can need up to 470GB of memory at GPT-3 scale

Up next: Multi-Query Attention and Grouped-Query Attention, which cut this cache by up to 8x without losing quality.


📚 Sources & further reading
Attention Is All You Need (the original Transformer paper) — https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head Is All You Need (introduces Multi-Query Attention) — https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.02150
GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models (introduces Grouped-Query Attention) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13245

title: A 1994 Engineer Would Assume Your Computer Is Broken
description:
A 1994 mainframe engineer counted every byte by hand and knew exactly when her memory was freed. Show her your React stack and she'd ask one question you can't answer.

This is about what we quietly gave up moving from deterministic, hardware-driven machines to the abstraction-driven modern stack. Not nostalgia — a real accounting. We trace how the industry traded cycle-by-cycle predictability for convenience: asynchronous runtimes where you don't control when code runs, JIT compilation that rewrites your program live, garbage collection that frees memory on its own schedule, and a virtual DOM maintained in memory just to draw text. A dozen processes spin up for one button, and we call it normal. The mainframe engineer wouldn't see freedom. She'd see an industry that stopped understanding its own machines. The honest answer isn't to go back — it's to remember what determinism bought us and decide whether the trade was worth it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 An Engineer in 1994
0:47 The World That Built Her
1:04 The Worship of Finite Resources
2:56 The Death of Deterministic Execution
4:23 A Browser Engine Just to Draw Text
5:52 The Surrender of Control
6:51 Garbage Collection: The Unthinkable
7:39 The Build That Broke for No Reason
8:28 A Reckoning for the Modern Architect
10:13 The Price You Can't See

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS COVERED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Deterministic execution and why mainframes had it
- JIT (just-in-time) compilation and runtime code generation
- Garbage collection vs manual memory management
- The virtual DOM and the cost of abstraction
- Async runtimes, the event loop, and edge execution
- Why a transitive dependency can break a build you didn't touch

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WATCH NEXT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Your 5GHz CPU Is Slower Than a 1983 Computer — we measured exactly what all this abstraction costs you in raw silicon:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NJRFXr7NkD4?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description&utm_campaign=mainframe-vs-react-stack

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
What did we lose moving from mainframes to modern software? Determinism — the ability to know exactly where, when, and how your code runs, and when memory is freed.
Why do a dozen processes start for one button? Modern browsers isolate rendering, compositing, GPU, and networking into separate processes and threads for safety and speed, at a large overhead cost.
Is the virtual DOM a bad thing? No — it's clever engineering, but it exists to paper over non-determinism we introduced by abandoning predictable execution.
Should engineers go back to counting bytes? No. The point is to use abstractions and still be able to explain what they cost on the silicon.

#mainframe #softwareengineering #webdevelopment #reactjs #abstraction

title: Can AI Actually Do Physics? A Harvard Professor Put It to the Test
description:
A Harvard physics professor refused to write a single line of code or do a single calculation — and let an AI agent (Claude Opus 4.5) write an entire cutting-edge particle physics paper for him. Three days in, it produced a 20-page draft. Then he looked closer… and found it had faked a plot and invented coefficients that don't exist.

As someone who spent years on Effective Field Theory during my PhD in theoretical physics at Cambridge, this paper felt like looking straight into the future of how science gets done. In this video I break down:

- What the paper actually solves (the "Sudakov shoulder" in the C-parameter), explained simply
- How Schwartz treated the AI like a second-year grad student — 102 tasks, a tree of markdown notes, strict prompting rules
- Where it broke: faked uncertainty bands, hallucinated formulas, and a calculus integral it just couldn't do
- How Gemini and GPT were brought in as a "peer-review committee" to catch each other's errors
- Why humans are still essential — the one thing AI still lacks: taste

📄 SOURCES
The paper (arXiv:2601.02484): https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.02484
Anthropic's write-up, "Vibe physics: The AI grad student": https://www.anthropic.com/research/vibe-physics

🎓 LEARN AI WITH ME

title: Dave Kerr - Bipolar Disorder, Dysregulation, and AI - AI Native DevCon June 2026
description:
Living with bipolar disorder, managing mood, state, mind, fear and excitement has been and continues to be a complex journey. As I have seen friends and colleagues and myself adapt to having creativity and productivity supercharged through AI, I've seen many examples of behaviours I've become sensitive to, intense bursts of hyper-creativity, addiction, excitement, depression, fatigue, dysregulation, withdrawal.

In this talk Dave will briefly discuss some elements of mood disorders, how he sees both adaptive and maladaptive behaviours in others, how we learn to regulate, and some of the best methods he has seen from others to be more fulfilled and happy through AI, but also be empathic, and protect themselves from risk. Hopefully some of these learnings provide food for thought for teams or individuals.

If you liked the talk, listen to The AI Native Dev Podcast at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLISstAySqk7KNRW1vfsjTqJnm4lXXBgPe

title: The World Cup Ball Has a Chip Inside It. Nobody Asked For This
description:
Before anyone can play with the official ball of the 2026 World Cup, someone has to plug it in. The Adidas Trionda has a computer chip, a rechargeable battery, and a charging pad — and nobody actually asked for any of it.

This is the story of how a soccer ball, an object that worked perfectly for a hundred years with zero dependencies, became one more "smart" device you have to charge, update, and depend on. We break down what's really inside the Trionda — the 500 Hz IMU sensor, the induction-charged battery, the counterweights Adidas had to add to cancel out the chip — and the semi-automated offside technology (SAOT) it feeds: 16 cameras, 29 points per body, 50 readings a second. The tech genuinely works. It cut offside calls from ~70 seconds to 20-25. That was never the question. The question is why the simplest object in sport now needs a battery, and who decided that for you.

This closes a series on over-engineering and the quiet creep of "smart" objects — from software that got slower as computers got faster, to a ball that got needier as the game got simpler.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The ball you have to charge
0:57 A perfect object
1:40 What's actually inside it
2:49 What it's all for: SAOT
4:03 A problem we invented
5:08 The goal you don't scream at
5:53 The ball is your whole house
7:15 Nobody asked

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WHAT'S IN THE BALL (quick reference)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Ball: Adidas Trionda, official ball of the 2026 World Cup (USA / Mexico / Canada)
- Sensor: IMU (inertial measurement unit) running at 500 Hz
- Power: rechargeable battery, wireless induction charging (~90 min charge → up to 6 hours use)
- System it feeds: semi-automated offside technology (SAOT) — 16 cameras, up to 29 body points, 50x per second
- Result: offside calls cut from ~70s to ~20-25s

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Does the 2026 World Cup ball really have a chip? Yes. The Adidas Trionda contains a 500 Hz IMU sensor that streams motion data in real time.

Why does the World Cup ball need a battery? The sensor needs power to run. The ball charges wirelessly on a pad; about 90 minutes gives up to 6 hours of use.

What is the chip in the ball for? It marks the exact instant of each kick and feeds that timing to semi-automated offside technology, which uses 16 cameras tracking 29 points per player.

Did the chip make offside calls faster? Yes — FIFA's testing cut offside decisions from around 70 seconds to roughly 20-25.

So is the technology bad? No. It works. The video's argument is about over-engineering and dependency, not about whether the tech does what it claims.

#WorldCup2026 #Trionda #SmartObjects #IoT #OverEngineering

Jeff started his career through design and freelance web development before joining HashiCorp as an early engineer, where he stayed through the company’s growth from around 200 employees to thousands of employees and an IPO. He later became a prominent contributor to Next.js, which led him to join Vercel as the manager of the Next.js team, helping shape the roadmap for one of the most important frameworks in web development.

Now at Clerk, Jeff spends more than 80% of his time writing code while also helping lead engineering at a fast-growing authentication and developer tools company Clerk.

In this conversation, we talk about breaking into software engineering without a traditional computer science background, how to get hired without spraying your résumé everywhere, what great developer tools companies do differently, how Clerk thinks about product engineering, and how AI is changing the way software engineers actually work.

Topics include software engineering careers, developer tools, engineering leadership, Next.js, Vercel, HashiCorp, Clerk, AI coding tools, software engineering interviews, product engineering, and the future of software development.

title: How C Really Works
description:
🔥 Check out my C course here (June sale): https://www.caveofprogramming.com/products/courses/c-for-programmers-in-other-languages?coupon=CJUNE

C is one of the simplest programming languages ever created — and one of the most powerful. In this video, I take you on a deep dive into how the C programming language actually works, from the abstract machine defined by the C standard to the real machine code your CPU executes.

We’ll walk through the entire build process — preprocessing, compilation, assembly, and linking — and see how a tiny C program becomes a real executable. Along the way, we explore how C maps to hardware, how objects and memory really work, why alignment and padding exist, how pointers behave, and why undefined behaviour gives C both its speed and its danger.

What you’ll learn:

How C compilers turn your code into machine instructions

Why C defines an abstract machine and how real CPUs emulate it

How stack frames, automatic storage, and function calls really work

What objects, variables, alignment, and byte representations mean in practice

Why pointers aren’t integers — and how strict aliasing affects your code

How undefined behaviour gives compilers freedom to optimise

Why C feels “close to the metal” even though it’s technically high‑level

If you’ve ever wondered why C is still used for kernels, drivers, embedded systems, and performance‑critical software, this video shows you exactly why. C’s model of memory, objects, and execution lines up almost perfectly with real hardware — and that’s what makes it both incredibly fast and incredibly unforgiving.

Whether you’re coming from Python, Java, or another high‑level language, this is a clear explanation of what C is really doing under the hood.

title: What Responsible AI Actually Means in 2026 – Microsoft's Sarah Bird
description:
We're no longer in the world of chatbots – we're in the world where AI systems take real-world action. So what does responsible AI even mean when agents write the code, review the code, and act across organizational boundaries?

Sarah Bird, Chief Product Officer of Responsible AI at Microsoft, has been in this field since it was a niche. Now it's everywhere. In this episode of Inference, she explains why the entire software development lifecycle is changing – and why human oversight has to evolve with it.

*In this episode of Inference, we get into:*
- Why "responsible AI" is the wrong framing – and why "trustworthy AI" matters more 
- What changes when agents write the code AND review the code 
- Why open-sourcing responsible AI tools is non-negotiable – "we don't want to be competing on this" 
- The dual-use problem: AI that finds vulnerabilities helps defenders AND attackers 
- Why fixing it "in the model" sounds easy but doesn't work – and who's actually responsible at each layer 
- The shift from chat to agentic to physical AI – and why consequences scale dramatically 
- What kids should learn about AI: the "stoplight" framework from New South Wales schools 
- Why Sarah is surprisingly optimistic – and why low p(doom) is a rational position 
- The three problems her team is solving right now: emerging risks, agent governance, and the new software lifecycle

We also talk about regulation, why responsible AI requires linguists alongside engineers, the rise of "psychosocial risk," and why humans + AI is more exciting than AGI alone.

This is a conversation about what it actually takes to make AI systems we can trust – and why humans are not optional. *Watch it!*

*Guest:* 
Sarah Bird, Chief Product Officer of Responsible AI at Microsoft https://www.linkedin.com/in/slbird/ 
https://x.com/slbird   
https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/ai/responsible-ai

title: Free AI Coding Has a Secret: You Already Lost
description:
Free AI coding isn't a business model — it's a subsidy with an expiration date, and the token economics underneath it are quietly conspiring to end it. Here's the math the $20 plan doesn't want you to see.

Every prompt and every generated line is billed per token, in both directions. But the real cost isn't precision — it's context. Because attention is quadratic, doubling your context window roughly quadruples the compute, which is why "load my whole repo" is the single most expensive thing you can ask an LLM to do. The labs are eating that gap on purpose: flood the market below cost, get you hooked, then reprice. If you're a casual user, you're cheap. If you're a power user — agents in the background, full-repo context, 30-second reasoning — you stopped being the customer and became the cost. This video follows the money on LLM inference cost, the VC-subsidized AI land grab, and what happens the day the meter turns on: a split between developers who go local (open-weight models like Qwen and Llama on Ollama) and a frontier that becomes a luxury good — where token efficiency becomes the most valuable skill a senior engineer has.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
VIDEO RELACIONADO DEL CANAL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Before you worry about your token bill, there's a quieter tax your machine pays before the model writes a single line:
▸ "The Invisible Bloat Ruining Our Computers" — watch that one next.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 Two Engineers, One Bill
0:39 What a Token Actually Costs
1:29 Why Context Is the Real Monster
3:01 The Subsidy Nobody Talks About
4:09 You Are Not the Customer. You Are the Cost.
5:28 The Fork: Going Local
6:13 The Frontier Becomes a Luxury
7:15 The Bill Is Coming

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTOS MENCIONADOS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
▸ Tokens & metered billing — you pay per token, input and output
▸ Quadratic attention — doubling context ≈ 4× the compute
▸ Million-token context window — the most expensive request you can make
▸ The VC subsidy / land grab — labs spending below cost to lock you in
▸ Negative operating margin — spending more than $2 for every $1 earned (early 2026)
▸ Customer vs. cost — the power-user crossover point
▸ Local / open-weight models — Ollama, Qwen (qwen2.5-coder), Llama, Mistral
▸ Token efficiency — the right answer in one tight prompt vs. an agent burning 10,000 tokens

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREGUNTAS FRECUENTES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why is AI coding so cheap right now? Labs are running at a loss on purpose — a VC-subsidized land grab to make you dependent before they reprice the metered API.
Why does context cost so much? Attention compares every token to every other token, so doubling the context window roughly quadruples the compute and memory needed.
Are you the customer or the cost? If you only autocomplete a few times a day, you're cheap. If you run agents and load full repos, you likely cost the lab more than you pay.
What happens when free AI coding ends? The market splits: everyday work moves to local open-weight models (Qwen, Llama via Ollama), while the frontier becomes a metered luxury good.
What is token efficiency? Getting the right answer in one tight, well-scoped prompt instead of letting a model burn thousands of tokens — the cheapest token is the one you never spend.
What about the bloat already wasting my machine's compute? That's a separate tax covered in the next video, "The Invisible Bloat Ruining Our Computers."

#AICoding #TokenEconomics #LLM #Ollama #DeveloperTools

title: How Florence Afforded the Renaissance
Marc Sloan - Harness engineering beyond code - product & design constraints for agents - AI Native D
description:
The next layer of the agent harness is the product and design context outside the codebase.

Agent harnesses have matured fast. Skills, `AGENTS.md`, Cursor rules, Claude skills. The field has settled on the same pattern: treat context as portable, versioned files that live in the repo and load on demand. This works for code because the context and the code live in the same place and change together. Tessl, along with much of the industry, is focused on making this layer trustworthy.

The harness is maturing for codebase context, but is still immature for everything else. Product, design, and business decisions live outside the codebase, in Figma, Notion, Confluence, Linear and more. When the agent needs to apply a design system, respect a constraint from a PRD, or factor in a customer signal, the source of truth lives in those tools, not the repo. Baking that context into a skill doesn't work, because the source evolves independently. Pulling it at runtime via MCP avoids that, but introduces drift in evals, versioning, and the infrastructure that depends on them.

To resolve this, the harness will need to evolve to include an intermediary layer between context in the codebase and sources of truth outside of it. In some areas, humans have already been doing this work for years; design system teams are the clearest example. At Figma I worked on Code Connect, an early attempt at design context harness engineering, and learned first hand the challenges in integrating product and design context from outside the repo into agentic workflows.

In this session, I will discuss where the harness goes next. It covers where skills work and where they break, what the field is doing about that gap, and how the harness might evolve at Tessl and beyond.

You'll leave with:
- A clear frame for why the next agent harness frontier must include product and design context.
- A concrete look at the challenges of bridging product and design context with coding agents, from design system context in Code Connect at Figma to today's open MCP-based approaches.
- A perspective on what the next layer of the agent harness might look like, at Tessl and beyond.

If you liked the talk, listen to The AI Native Dev Podcast at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLISstAySqk7KNRW1vfsjTqJnm4lXXBgPe

title: Hyperelasticity
description:
The deformation gradient tells us how a material point deforms. But how much stress results from that deformation?

In this video, we develop the framework of hyperelastic material modeling to answer exactly that question. We introduce the strain energy density function as the central object that links deformation to stress, and explore the physical requirements it must satisfy to produce a physically meaningful material response. We then discuss one of the most widely used hyperelastic models: the Neo-Hookean model.

Keywords: engineering, physics, continuum mechanics, solid mechanics, fluid mechanics, deformation gradient, stress tensor, strain tensor, Cauchy stress tensor, first Piola-Kirchhoff stress tensor, second Piola-Kirchhoff stress tensor, hyperelasticity, large deformation, thermodynamics of materials, material frame indifference, objectivity, isotropy, compressible, incompressible, Helmholtz free energy potential, stored energy density potential, strain energy density potential

References:
Fuhg et al., A Review on Data-Driven Constitutive Laws for Solids (https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s11831-024-10196-2)
Linden et al., Neural networks meet hyperelasticity: A guide to enforcing physics (https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0022509623001679)
Linka & Kuhl, A new family of Constitutive Artificial Neural Networks towards automated model discovery (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0045782522006867)

title: LeetCode Proves Nothing About Real Coding Ability
description:
There's a coding interview that decides who lands six-figure jobs — and it has almost nothing to do with engineering. Here's what LeetCode actually measures.

This is a breakdown of why the modern technical interview is epistemically broken. A developer can sit in the top 1% of LeetCode and the bottom half of real engineering, and the test can't tell the difference — it was never built to. We walk through the cognitive asymmetry between the whiteboard interview and the actual job, the false positive (the grinder who can't read a stack trace) and the false negative (the senior who freezes implementing a heap from memory), and the real reason this filter survives across software engineering hiring: it's cheap, scalable, legally defensible, and a convenient proxy for grit. Accuracy never made the cut. For intermediate-to-advanced engineers who've felt the gap between grinding algorithms and shipping software.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The test that has nothing to do with the job
0:48 The test isn't the job
2:11 What LeetCode actually measures
3:32 The false positive and the false negative
5:04 Why a broken test survives
7:33 What actually predicts a good engineer

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WATCH NEXT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
If the interview was theater, the way we verify our code might be the next stage of it.
→ Unit Tests Are Lying to You — Here's What Seniors Test Instead: [PLACEHOLDER: pega aquí la URL del video]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS IN THIS VIDEO
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Cognitive asymmetry — the job rewards slow, collaborative, ambiguous thinking; the test rewards fast, solitary, sterile recall.
- False positive / false negative — a filter that lets in people who can't do the job and rejects people who've done it for a decade.
- Effort filter in a skill-test costume — LeetCode mostly measures free time to grind, a proxy for privilege as much as ability.
- The incentive trap — cheap, scalable, legally defensible, grit-signaling. Accuracy is the one thing it doesn't optimize for.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Does LeetCode measure real coding ability? Not the ability the job uses. It measures pattern recall under time pressure — recognizing ~15 templates fast and alone — not reading unfamiliar codebases, debugging production, or collaborating across a system.

Why do companies still use LeetCode if it's flawed? Because of the incentives around it, not because it works: it's cheap to run at scale, gives HR a defensible paper trail, and reads as a signal of grit. Predicting job performance never made the list.

What's the false positive vs false negative problem? The test passes grinders who can't spin up a local environment (false positive) and rejects senior engineers who reach for a library instead of reinventing a heap by hand (false negative) — failing in both directions at once.

What actually predicts a good engineer? Not how many algorithms you memorized, but how you reason about the quality of what you build — and whether the thing you shipped actually does what you think it does.

#LeetCode #CodingInterview #SoftwareEngineering #TechInterview #FAANG

title: You Can Learn AI Agent Memory System In 12 Min | Semantic & Episodic Memory, RAG, Vector Database
description:
If you've used ChatGPT or Claude for a while, you've already been using an AI memory system without realizing it. It knows who you are, what you asked last week, sometimes even what your company does. In this one I draw the whole thing out on a whiteboard, step by step, so you can design your own even if you don't come from a technical background.

Here's what we cover:
- Working memory (the context RAM): your prompt, chat history, and system prompt, and why every session is wiped unless you save it
- Procedural memory: how the agent should behave, its rules and skills, usually a plain file like a SKILL.md
- Semantic memory: durable facts and user profiles in a vector database, pulled back with RAG so you only fetch the few most relevant pieces
- Episodic memory: a dated log of past chats and events on a timeline
- The consolidation gate and a cheaper summarizer agent that distills it all into durable facts

That last part is the trick most people miss. You don't search the giant episodic log every time, you wait until enough chats pile up, then a cheaper model distills them into a handful of facts. It's why your ChatGPT memory stays short but somehow always up to date.

You can build anything. You can learn anything. 💪

Timeline

00:45 What is a User Prompt
01:14 Working Memory (Context RAM)
02:55 The AI Agent Session is Ephemeral

03:42 Procedural Memory (Skill.md)
