title: The data black hole at the center of AI
description:
It is easy to forget how much data these models are trained on, and how much more it is than what we humans see in our lifetimes. We see these AIs as a galaxy glittering with capabilities, but at their center, invisible to the naked eye, holding all the constellations together, is an unimaginably massive black hole of data.

Thanks to Mercury for sponsoring this essay. Mercury is my banking platform, and they just released a new AI feature called Command. Since I already use Mercury to run basically my entire business, Command has access to all the info it needs to get real work done. I can ask it to send invoices, or categorize expenses, or even transfer money… and Command just handles it. Learn more at https://mercury.com/command

Read the transcript here:
https://www.dwarkesh.com/p/the-sample-efficiency-black-hole-2

title: מה באמת קורה כשאתם מקלידים כתובת בדפדפן? | סרטון AI מטורף
description:
מה באמת קורה מהרגע שאנחנו מקלידים כתובת אתר בדפדפן?

בסרטון הזה בניתי הדמיית AI מלאה שמציגה את המסע של Packet באינטרנט — מהדפדפן שלכם ועד לשרת המרוחק, וחזרה.

title: Indian Companies Are Desperately Hiring But Nobody Is Applying
description:
India has a 51% AI talent gap and over 6 lakh open data science roles right now. These are the 5 highest-paying careers in India for 2026, and exactly how to get into them, backed by real salary data, real company stories, and real demand numbers.

If you are 18 to 35, working a white-collar job, and wondering whether there is a smarter, higher-income move out there, this career guide is for you.

In this video we break down 5 high-paying, future-proof careers exploding in India right now:
Data Scientist: Use data to predict what a business does next. Salary ₹6 LPA to ₹55 LPA+, with 6 lakh+ open roles and a 51% supply gap.

AI and Machine Learning Engineer: The fastest-growing job title of 2026. Salary ₹4.2 LPA to ₹95 LPA+, with a 3.3 to 1 demand-to-supply ratio in India.

Cybersecurity Specialist: India needs 1 million professionals and has only 80,000. Salary ₹4 LPA to ₹35 LPA+. Certifications matter more than your degree.

Cloud Infrastructure Manager: The foundation every company runs on. Salary ₹6 LPA to ₹1 Crore+ at hyperscalers. India's cloud market is set to 4x by 2033.

Supply Chain Manager: The operations backbone of India's e-commerce and quick-commerce boom. Salary ₹3 LPA to ₹30 LPA+.

For each career you get, what they actually do all day, what real companies like Amazon, Netflix, JPMorgan, Disney+, Swiggy and Flipkart pay for, the salary path from fresher to senior, the qualifications and certifications you need, and how big the opportunity really is in India.

The biggest gap between people who break into these careers and people who don't is not talent. It is information. Now you have it.

title: You Don’t Need That Design Pattern
description:
🧱 Build software that lasts. Join the Software Design Mastery waiting list → https://arjan.codes/mastery.

Design patterns aren't bad. But using them too early can make your code more complicated than it needs to be.

In this video, I start with a Python example that uses inheritance, strategies, and a factory to solve a very small problem. Then I simplify it and explore a more realistic scenario where an abstraction actually provides value. Along the way, I look at a simple rule of thumb for deciding when a design pattern helps and when it just adds complexity.

If you've ever wondered whether you're over-engineering your code, this video is for you.

title: Hermes Agent Masterclass: 7. Cron & Automation
description:
Module 7 of the Hermes Agent Masterclass: Cron cuts the last cord — your agent runs itself on a schedule, in a fresh session, and delivers to your phone. And I'll show you how to make it cost almost nothing.

Sign up for my FREE weekly newsletter, where I spill my unfiltered thoughts on the latest AI news, cool research, and projects I'm building: https://www.onchainaigarage.com/

🐦 Follow Tonbi on X for real-time AI x blockchain updates! https://x.com/tonbistudio

Module 7 is all about Cron and automation — running Hermes Agent on a schedule with no one in the loop. I cover how Cron actually works (it lives in the always-on gateway, which ticks every 60 seconds and spins up a fresh, isolated session per job), the four ways to schedule a job (CLI, chat, TUI, and the web dashboard with one-click blueprints), and how to deliver the result anywhere — or nowhere, using the "silent" keyword. The best part is the cost engineering: no_agent mode runs a pure script for $0, and the wake-agent gate polls cheaply every couple minutes and only pays for the model when state actually changes — which I demo live with a real bug-triage job on my Agent Wikis app. Finally I chain jobs into a real pipeline with context-from (no framework needed) and build a daily traffic briefing that collects data for free all day and only spends a cent on the part a human reads.

Resources:
🔗 Hermes Agent: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent
🔗 Agent Wikis (used in the live demos): https://agentwikis.com/

Timestamps:
0:00 - Module 7: cutting the last cord with Cron
2:19 - How Cron works: the gateway tick + fresh sessions
4:06 - The 4 ways to schedule a job
6:21 - Schedule formats + the no-self-spawn safety rail
9:10 - Deliver anywhere + the "silent" keyword
11:18 - The #1 Cron mistake: fresh sessions start from zero
12:39 - Spend nothing: no_agent mode (RAM watcher)
14:50 - The wake-agent gate (live bug-triage demo)
23:12 - More cost layers: rerun scripts, lean tool sets, resilience
25:30 - Chaining jobs with context-from (daily briefing build)

What would you point a Cron job at — a daily brief, a monitor, a whole pipeline? Let me know in the comments. Module 8 is on sub-agents and the Kanban board: running many agents at once. Subscribe and hit the bell so you don't miss it! 🦐✨

title: The AI Chip Shortage Is a Lie. Here's the Proof
description:
Everyone says the world is running out of AI chips. The truth is uglier: we're already throwing away nearly half of every chip we own. The AI chip shortage is mostly a software-efficiency problem wearing a hardware costume.

This is a deep-dive into why frontier GPUs deliver only 35-55% of their real capability (the number engineers call Model FLOP Utilization), why the actual 2026 bottleneck is electrical — transformers, switchgear, power — not silicon, and why the cure isn't a bigger purchase order. It's mechanical sympathy: building software from the metal up instead of the convenience down.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO FROM THE CHANNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The same physics that's burning half a trillion dollars in data centers is sitting on your own desk. Watch: "Your 5GHz CPU Is Slower Than a 1983 Computer"

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 Introduction: A Chip at 37%
1:01 The $700 Billion Headline
1:31 The Bottleneck Already Moved (Power, Not Chips)
3:27 The Hidden Cost of Abstraction
5:18 Adding Machines Doesn't Fix Waiting
6:13 Mechanical Sympathy: Working With the Metal
7:24 Digital Blacksmiths Beat the Cluster
8:08 The Return to Systems Languages
9:15 DeepSeek and the Lean-Model Shift
10:49 Abundance Through Efficiency
12:20 What This Means for Your Own Machine

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Model FLOP Utilization (MFU): real-world measure showing frontier GPUs run at ~35-55% of peak.
- The electrical bottleneck: transformers, switchgear and batteries — not chips — gating 2026 buildout.
- Cache vs main memory: the ~100:1 latency penalty that determines real performance.
- Mechanical sympathy: writing code that respects the physical layout of the chip (term from F1 driver Jackie Stewart).
- Data-oriented design: flat, contiguous arrays over scattered objects so the processor never stalls.
- Systems languages / compile-to-the-metal: erasing abstraction at compile time for safety and speed at once.
- Distillation & lean models: how DeepSeek trained frontier-class at a fraction of the compute.
- "Digital blacksmiths": engineers who build from the silicon up.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Is the AI chip shortage real? Partly. You genuinely can't buy enough GPUs right now, but the binding constraint has moved to electrical equipment (transformers, switchgear, power), and a large share of installed compute already runs at ~40-55% utilization — so much of the "shortage" is wasted capacity, not missing silicon.

Why do AI GPUs run at only 40%? Because software stalls them. Garbage collection, virtualization, and layers of indirection scatter data across memory, forcing the chip to wait on ~100:1 memory-latency penalties instead of computing. The fix is software discipline, not more hardware.

What is mechanical sympathy in programming? A term borrowed from F1 driver Jackie Stewart: writing code that respects the chip as a physical object — keeping data contiguous and cache-resident so the processor almost never stalls.

How did DeepSeek change the assumption? It trained a frontier-class model at a fraction of the usual compute using a distillation-heavy approach, shifting the question from "how big can we make it" to "how little silicon can it need."

Does this mean frameworks are bad? No. Most software can afford to be slow. The waste only matters where performance is load-bearing — the frontier training and inference the world is spending three quarters of a trillion dollars on.

#AIChipShortage #SoftwareEfficiency #MechanicalSympathy #SystemsProgramming #ProgrammingDeepDive

title: Fable 5: The Full Story from Capabilities to Drama (Ep. 1002 with Jon Krohn)
uploader: Super Data Science: ML & AI Podcast with Jon Krohn
description:
Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 was the most capable AI model ever released to the public and it lasted just three days before the US government forced it offline. @JonKrohnLearns unpacks both halves of the story: what makes Fable 5 special, and why it was pulled. Fable 5 and its locked-down sibling Mythos 5 are the same model separated only by safeguards, in a new “Mythos-class” tier above Opus. Jon covers its state-of-the-art benchmarks, premium $10/$50-per-million-token pricing, conservative safety classifiers, and the federal export-control directive, reportedly sparked by an Amazon-flagged “jailbreak” that took it down.

Additional materials: https://www.superdatascience.com/1002

title: Microsoft’s new AI models & bots dominate the internet
description:
Visit Mixture of Experts podcast page to get more AI content → https://ibm.biz/~P2MwO1Liq

Are AI agents taking over the internet? This week on Mixture of Experts, Tim Hwang is joined by Ambhi Ganesan, Rynne Whitnah and Sandi Besen. First, a Cloudflare study reveals that agentic AI bots now generate 57.4% of web requests globally, with humans accounting for just 42.6%. Our experts debate whether this signals a fundamental shift in how we interact with the web—or if bots have dominated internet traffic all along. Next, Microsoft makes a major play in the AI model race, releasing MAI-Thinking-1, a trillion-parameter mixture of experts model, and MAI-Image-1 for image generation. Finally, AI-generated films debut at the Tribeca Film Festival. Our experts discuss the evolution of filmmaking, the role of AI as a creative tool, and whether we’ll see AI films win critical awards in the future. All that and more on this week’s Mixture of Experts.

title: Ryan Lopopolo - Harness Engineering: How to Build Software When Humans Steer and Agents Execute
description:
Hear more from Ryan on the AI Native Dev Podcast: https://youtu.be/MFQIKbr1IEo

Reasoning models and coding agents have changed what is possible in software development, but most teams are still using workflows built for a world where humans write nearly all the code directly. That mismatch is where much of the disappointment around AI coding comes from.

This talk introduces Harness Engineering: an agent-first approach to building software where the key engineering task shifts from manually producing every implementation detail to designing the harnesses that let agents do useful work safely, repeatedly, and at high quality.

Ryan shares practical patterns for making AI coding actually work in production: how to scope autonomy, shape context, design eval and verification loops, structure tool access, and build feedback systems that keep agents effective without making them useless. This talk also covers where agents are already strong, where they still fail, and why 'just give the model the repo' is not a serious deployment strategy.

If you are trying to move from demos to real delivery, this talk offers a concrete framework for how agent-first engineering actually works.

title: LLM Interview Series #4: What is Attention?
description:
==========================================================
Preparing for AI, ML, or LLM infrastructure interviews? Practice real interview-style questions here:
https://interview.vizuara.ai/
==========================================================

LLM Interview Series #4: What Is Attention?

“What is attention?” is one of the most important interview questions in deep learning and LLMs.

Most candidates can say that attention helps a model “focus on important tokens.”

But that is only the surface-level answer.

In this video, we go deeper and build the attention mechanism from scratch:

- Why do we need attention conceptually?
- What problem does attention solve in sequence modeling?
- How do queries, keys, and values work?
- What matrix multiplications are involved?
- What does QKᵀ actually compute?
- How do attention weights interact with values?
- What is the difference between single-head and multi-head attention?

We also talk about how to answer this in an interview with multiple levels of depth. A strong answer is not just a definition. It should show first-principles thinking, matrix-level understanding, and genuine passion for why this idea changed modern AI.

The goal is to move beyond memorized explanations and lead the interview discussion with clarity, curiosity, and depth.

==========================================================
Preparing for AI, ML, or LLM infrastructure interviews? Practice real interview-style questions here:
https://interview.vizuara.ai/
==========================================================

#LLMInterview #AttentionMechanism #Transformer #AIInterview #MachineLearning

title: 00b - Microscopy in Drug Discovery: From Cells to Tissues to the Future | AI for Drug Discovery
description:
Microscopy is not a single-stage tool in drug discovery, it shows up at every stage of the pipeline, from identifying where a protein target lives inside a cell, to measuring how far a drug penetrates into tumor tissue, to reading biopsy slides in clinical trials. This video maps every major microscopy insertion point across all seven stages of drug discovery, covers what is current, and previews where the field is heading.

Topics covered: confocal and super-resolution microscopy for target identification, cryo-EM for structure-based drug design, Cell Painting and high-content screening, zebrafish xenograft imaging, 2D vs 3D cell culture and organoid imaging, light sheet and lightfield microscopy, spatial pharmacokinetics, intravital microscopy, digital pathology and AI, tissue clearing, spatial transcriptomics, CODEX multiplexed imaging, and emerging tools including SRS microscopy, expansion microscopy, and organ-on-chip.

Link to code: https://github.com/bnsreenu/AI-for-Drug-Discovery

title: Vectors all the way down: AI Mental Model #4
description:
==============================================================
📓 Free visual lecture notes for this episode:
https://vizuaraai.github.io/great-mental-models-of-ai/lecture-04-vectors-all-the-way-down.html
==============================================================

Every AI model gets the fame. But before a model can process text, images, audio, or video, something quieter has to happen first: the world must become vectors.

This is Lecture 4 of The Great Mental Models of Artificial Intelligence. In this episode, we explore one of the deepest ideas underneath modern AI: everything a model sees is first turned into lists of numbers.

Text becomes tokens and embeddings. Images become pixels and patches. Audio becomes waveforms, codec tokens, and latent vectors. Video becomes spacetime patches. Different media, same trick: turn the world into vectors so the machine can add, multiply, and learn.

In this lecture, we look at:

(1) Why computers only speak numbers
(2) How text becomes tokens and embedding vectors
(3) Why tokenization quietly shapes what a language model can learn
(4) How images become pixel grids and patch tokens
(5) How audio becomes codec tokens through neural compression
(6) How video becomes spacetime patches
(7) Why the vectorization step happens before the model ever runs
(8) Why many breakthroughs come from better representations, not just bigger models

#ArtificialIntelligence #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #Vectors #Embeddings #Tokenization #LLM #MultimodalAI #Vizuara

description:
Olivier Lacombe, Director of Product Management for Gemma, sits down with Gus Martins and Alice Zheng to dig into what Gemma 4 unlocks inside the Google AI Edge Gallery app.  Alice walks through the app's growth since launch, more than five million model downloads in the first month, and demos two of the most popular use cases: asking Gemma to explain why the sky is blue completely offline, and using audio input to generate a structured shopping list on the spot.  Gus covers the agent skills framework and what the community is building with it, from Wikipedia and WebFetch integrations to HTML rendering and mini games.  They also preview MCP integration coming to Android first and then iOS, persistent chat history, and what it means that the 2B model this year is matching the performance of last year's 27B dense.

Resources: 
Google AI Edge Gallery for Android → https://goo.gle/4vmF2Op 
Google AI Edge Gallery for IOS → https://goo.gle/44jkpqk 
Explore Gemma → https://goo.gle/44jktX6 

The app is free, open source on GitHub, and available on the Play Store and App Store.

title: Infrastructure with AI Agents for Dummies
description:
AI agents are powerful force multipliers, but only if you know what you're doing. This video walks through the full journey of using an AI agent to manage real cloud infrastructure, starting with the worst possible approach (letting the agent run loose with CLI tools it found on your machine) and ending with a production-grade setup built on GitOps principles. Along the way, you'll see exactly why an agent happily creating a publicly accessible database with the password stored in a temp file isn't a success story. It's a disaster waiting to happen.

The video makes a compelling case for the right stack: Crossplane Managed Resources over imperative CLIs, Crossplane Compositions as the backbone of an Internal Developer Platform that hides complexity behind a clean interface, and Argo CD to enforce GitOps so that Git becomes the only entry point for any change. The core message is clear — AI doesn't replace the need to understand what you're building. It amplifies whatever you bring to the table. Use it to hide your ignorance, and you get chaos at scale. Use it to learn, ask why at every step, and you get something genuinely great.

title: Devs vs. AI Agents: Build Salesforce in 6 hours | The Greenfield Games S1.E3
description:
Presented by Replay (https://codetv.link/replay) — The promise of AI is that anyone can build anything at any time. But is build really better than buy? That’s what we’re here to find out. Join your host Kedasha Kerr for episode 3 of _The Greenfield Games_.

As a company grows, it becomes critical to keep track of leads, customers, and other user activity in a centralized place. Customer relationship management (CRM) tools like Salesforce and Hubspot are one of the first SaaS purchases many companies make, but they can become a huge cost center as you scale.

The challenge in episode is to build a CRM system. The devs are not going to be building a Salesforce clone; what we want to see is how teams can leverage modern tools to solve the same set of challenges Salesforce and Hubspot solve, but in a way that’s custom-tuned for their team’s working style.

title: How Easy Is It to Identify YOU on the Internet?
description:
Welcome to the MCS100 series, where I will explain 100 random topics in Math and Computer Science over 100 days. Today is Day x, and today’s topic is: How Easy Is It to Identify YOU on the Internet?

Identifying a person online is incredibly easy, often taking only a few minutes, provided the target leaves minor "digital breadcrumbs". Using Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT), a single username, email address, or photo can be cross-referenced across public records, social media, and data breaches to build a highly detailed identity profile.

title: Is There Really a 'Vienna School of Agentic Coding'?
description:
Is there really a Vienna School of Agentic Coding? Well no, not really.

So where did the term come from?

Following the quick adoption of OpenClaw and Pi, both created by Austrian developers, some corners of the internet began to wonder if something was happening over there in Austria.

And while the three developers to which the term was applied reject it, they do share some loose beliefs about how to build software in the AI age:

thinking is essential
minimalism beats maximalism
software should be malleable
tools should be open and neutral

This is a short introduction to the so called 'Vienna School of Agentic Coding.'
Featuring Armin Ronacher and Mario Zechner.

title: Why AI Labs With Unlimited GPUs Still Fail — Anjney Midha, AMP
description:
From building Discord’s developer platform and backing frontier AI companies like Anthropic, Mistral, Black Forest Labs, and Periodic Labs to now building AMP’s independent compute grid, Anjney Midha has spent years close to the real bottlenecks of AI scaling. In this episode, Anjney joins swyx at Periodic Labs to unpack why the AI race is not just about buying more GPUs, why 95% utilization would have been considered an outage at Google, and why the next era of AI infrastructure has to be more aligned, more efficient, and more responsible.

We go deep on AMP’s vision for a compute grid that makes FLOPs flow like megawatts, the difference between full-stack AI labs and horizontal pooling, why AI data centers need community buy-in, and how compute markets could evolve into something closer to an independent system operator. Anjney also explains why DeepMind’s unpublished research points to a market failure, why end-of-life prediction remains one of the most important AI applications he has thought about for fourteen years, and why “output maxing” may become a new discipline for frontier systems.

We also discuss Anthropic’s culture, why “luck favors the prepared mind” in coding models, how Claude cracked coding, why too much capital too early can make AI labs fragile, what Periodic Labs is trying to do with science and superconductors, why great researchers can become great CEOs, and why Silicon Valley is both deeply missionary and deeply mercenary.

We discuss:
• Why 95% utilization was considered an outage at Google
• Why AI infrastructure waste compounds at frontier-lab scale
• Why “move fast and break things” does not work for AI data centers
• How data center backlash, power grids, and community incentives shape AI scaling
• AMP’s vision for making FLOPs flow like megawatts
• Why compute needs an independent system operator
• How interruptible demand and dynamic prioritization worked inside Google
• Why DeepMind research being hoarded creates negative externalities
• AMP’s 1.2GW base-load ambition and the need for 6GW of spike capacity
• Why end-of-life prediction could become one of AI’s most important healthcare applications
• Frontier Systems, output maxing, and full-stack alignment
• Why APIs, abstraction layers, and organizations become lossy as they scale
• Superconductors, standards, and the dream of lossless systems
• SF Compute, open protocols, and the future of compute marketplaces
• Why non-NVIDIA chips can still benefit from NVIDIA’s reference architecture
• Trust boundaries and why chip startups need visibility into future model architectures
• Why VCs often underestimate researchers as CEOs
• Scientists as star athletes of the mind
• Why great CEOs need to be confrontational up and down the stack
• Why “winning” is less important than leading the frontier
• How Anthropic cracked coding
• Why culture is fragile, not a permanent moat
• Why hardship was a feature, not a bug, for Anthropic
• Why Anthropic’s P0 was coding from day one
• Periodic Labs, physics as the constraint, and technical reality
• Silicon Valley mercenaries, missionary teams, and what happens after a breakthrough

—

Anjney Midha
• LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/anjney/
• X: https://x.com/AnjneyMidha

title: Learn the Binary Search Algorithm in TypeScript from ThePrimeagen
description:
ThePrimeagen dives into the Binary Search algorithm, an efficient algorithm that finds an item in a sorted list by repeatedly halving the portion of the list that could contain the target. This process continues until the possible locations are narrowed down to a single one, making it far faster than checking each item individually. The final solution is implemented in TypeScript.

title: JudgmentBench: Comparing Rubric and Preference Evaluation for Quality Assessment
description:
Russell Yang of Stanford Law dives into his paper "JudgmentBench: Comparing Rubric and Preference Evaluation for Quality Assessment" at the Snorkel AI Reading Group in San Francisco.

Read the paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25240
Subscribe to be notified of future Reading Group and other learning events: https://luma.com/snorkel-ai

Follow Russell on:
LinkedIn:   / russell-yang  

CHAPTERS 
0:00 Introduction
1:40 Russell's intro & talk overview
1:58 Science vs. art: where does legal practice fall?
4:09 Two evaluation paradigms: rubrics vs. comparative judgment
5:25 Expert attorney annotators
6:37 BigLawBench & the JudgmentBench dataset
7:48 Constructing quality levels without ground truth
10:47 Key finding: comparative judgment wins
11:15 Two metrics: rank correlation & win rate
12:56 Results: 67% vs. 54% win rate, 0.908 vs. 0.150 Spearman's ρ
14:35 Implications for benchmarkers and law firms (buying tools & building tooling)
15:02 Implications for law firms (buying tools & building tooling)
15:14 What's next: auto-rubrics from comparative judgment data
16:09 Q&A

Russell covers the motivation for comparing rubric and preference evaluation, why ground truth is especially contested in legal domains, how JudgmentBench was constructed using 50+ practicing attorneys and Harvey's BigLawBench, the two metrics used to measure quality recovery, and the striking gap between comparative judgment and rubric-based scoring, along with the implications for model benchmarking, law firm tooling decisions, and the future of expert data collection.

title: The best AI agents are simpler than you think
description:
Zack Reneau-Wedeen is the Head of Product at Sierra, the conversational AI platform behind customer-facing agents for most of the Fortune 20. Before Sierra, he spent seven years at Google as the founding PM for Google Lens and Google Podcasts, then led product at Robinhood and CoinTracker. Sierra is mostly known for customer support, but Zack reveals how and why the company is building agents that span the entire customer lifecycle, from browsing and booking to sales and loyalty. In this conversation, he argues agentic commerce will be bigger than e-commerce, explains why he's a "monolith loyalist", and unpacks why, when a model looks dumb, the problem is usually you.

We also discuss:
• How Sierra's no-code layer compiles down to agent code, and back again
• Why most multi-agent systems just ship your org chart
• Inside Sierra's modular voice architecture: thinking, listening, and talking in parallel
• Why Sierra built a PCI-certified stack for voice payments
• How outcome-based pricing aligns incentives
• Why there's no breakout memory company

title: My Hermes Agent Runs My Web App on a $6 VPS (And Replaces Vercel & Railway)
description:
I replaced Vercel, Supabase, and Railway with one small VPS and a single Hermes agent that runs and maintains my live sites for me. Here's the whole setup.

Sign up for my FREE weekly newsletter, where I spill my unfiltered thoughts on the latest AI news, cool research, and projects I'm building: https://www.onchainaigarage.com/

🐦 Follow Tonbi on X for real-time AI x blockchain updates! https://x.com/tonbistudio

When you start vibe-coding apps, the tools push you toward Vercel, Supabase, and Railway — but I run everything on one small VPS with a Hermes agent instead, and I'll show you exactly how. Using my live site Agent Wikis (agentwikis.com — free knowledge bases for agents) as the example, I break down why git is the only "database" a docs/wiki/blog-class site needs, how the agent lives on the box and serves the site through Caddy, and the autonomous Cron pipeline that keeps every wiki fresh behind a single Telegram approval. Then I demo the new "demand loop": agents search the wikis via an MCP server, repeated misses (privacy-preserving, no prompts or IPs stored) become an authoring backlog, and the agent researches and fills those gaps automatically — live on the site in minutes. This is Part 1 (the site that needs no database); Part 2 deploys a game app that does need one, on the same box.

Resources:
🔗 Agent Wikis (the live site): https://agentwikis.com/
🔗 Hermes Agent: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent

title: The Production AI Playbook: Deploying Agents at Enterprise Scale — Sandipan Bhaumik, Databricks
description:
A retail bank spent £85,000 over six months on a chatbot PoC that could not reach production. No one could explain why it was failing. When Sandipan Bhaumik's team got involved, they picked the model in week seven of an eight-week engagement — the first six weeks went to evaluation data, tracing infrastructure, and a measurement pipeline. Six weeks post launch, when the bank updated its interest rate policy and customer satisfaction dropped, the tracing system caught the cause: the new policy document had not been reembedded and the agent was serving stale answers.

The talk covers the five pillars he built from that and similar engagements: evaluation (define success numerically before touching code), observability (trace every agent decision — European regulators require it), data foundation (agents do not forgive bad data the way humans do), multi agent orchestration patterns, and governance (47 PII breaches caught in testing before launch). The evaluation data set is a living system, not a fixed benchmark. The production incident playbook connects all five.

Speaker info:
- https://www.linkedin.com/in/sandipanbhaumik

title: 🎥 Statistics 4.4 – Combinations & Permutations | Counting Methods, Factorials & Special Cases
description:
Welcome to Chapter 4.4: Combinations and Permutations! In this lesson, we learn how to count the number of possible outcomes in a variety of situations — from arranging people in line to selecting committees, forming batting orders, and even rearranging letters in a word. These counting techniques are the backbone of probability and appear everywhere in statistics, computer science, and real‑world decision‑making.

We’ll cover:
✅ Factorials — what they are, how they work, and how to compute them in Excel
— Why 0!=1
— How factorials form the foundation of counting formulas
✅ Permutations — when order matters 𝑛𝑃𝑟=𝑛!/(𝑛−𝑟)!
— Examples: arranging students, forming batting orders, lining up friends
— How to compute permutations by hand and in Excel
✅ Combinations — when order does NOT matter  𝑛𝐶𝑟=𝑛!/(𝑟!(𝑛−𝑟)!)
— Examples: choosing committees, selecting vegetables, drawing colored blocks
— How combinations relate to permutations
✅ Real‑world probability applications  
— Probability of repeating a batting order
— Probability of drawing specific colored blocks
— Choosing officers, forming groups, and more
✅ Permutations with repetition  
— How to count arrangements when some objects are identical
— Example: rearranging the letters in TENNESSEE  
— Why repeated letters reduce the number of distinguishable arrangements
✅ Spreadsheet setup for factorials, permutations, combinations, and probability formatting

You’ll see how to decide whether order matters, how to choose the correct formula, and how to compute results efficiently. By the end of the video, you’ll be able to calculate permutations, combinations, and special permutations — and apply them to probability questions with confidence.

🎓 Perfect for:  
Introductory Statistics students
Business, social science, health, or STEM majors
Anyone learning how to count outcomes and compute probabilities in structured scenarios

title: The RAM Explosion Nobody Signed Off On | Linux Then vs Now
description:
In 2005 an entire Linux desktop booted into ~200MB of RAM. Today the "lightweight" replacement asks for 2GB — same word, ten times the memory, and nobody signed off on the change.

This is the story of how lightweight Linux stopped being a measurement and became a vibe. We trace the regression from KDE 3.5's disciplined ~200MB idle to a modern "minimal" Xubuntu that idles at 500-600MB and recommends 2GB — and we open the hood on where the weight actually went: bundled formats (Snap, Flatpak, AppImage), background daemons, GTK2-to-GTK3 abstraction layers, and web-integrated UI that spawns a real browser engine just to draw a sign-in form. The deeper loss is mechanical sympathy: software that no longer respects the machine it runs on.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives into how software really works. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO FROM THE CHANNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This video closes a trilogy on software inefficiency. The same instinct that turned a start menu into a browser engine turned almost every app you open into one too. Watch the companion:
▸ Every Desktop App Is Secretly a Browser (And It's Eating Your RAM)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Anomaly: 200MB vs 2GB
0:48 When Lightweight Was a Discipline
2:06 The Xfce Promise and the Xubuntu Pivot
3:47 Anatomy of Architectural Decay
5:37 The Bloat Floor: Putting Numbers to It
6:27 Why Your Start Menu Runs a Browser
7:18 Who Actually Pays for the Bloat
8:56 Bring Back the Ethos

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Mechanical sympathy — writing code aware of the machine underneath it
- The bloat floor — RAM the system reserves to stay responsive before you open anything
- Relative performance — "lighter than GNOME" replacing "light" as a measurement
- Shared vs bundled libraries — one resident copy vs Snap/Flatpak/AppImage carrying their own
- Background daemons — telemetry collectors, snapd, update pollers idling resident
- Web-integrated UI / captive-portal WebKit — a real browser engine to render a sign-in form
- Software bloat as an engine of electronic waste

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
How much RAM does Xubuntu actually use? A modern minimal Xubuntu install idles around 500-600MB, a full install around 700-800MB, and the recommended spec asks for 2GB to run several apps comfortably — versus a 2005-era lightweight desktop that idled near 200MB.

Is Xfce still lightweight in 2026? Xfce keeps its lean reputation, but it runs on a stack (GTK) that moved from GTK2 to GTK3 and added Wayland support, dragging in dependencies and background infrastructure. "Lightweight" today means lighter than GNOME, not light by measurement.

Why does lightweight Linux use so much RAM now? Bundled package formats (Snap, Flatpak, AppImage) load redundant copies of libraries, background daemons sit resident, and web-integrated UI components spawn browser-grade rendering engines for simple tasks. Each abstraction is reasonable alone; stacked, they forget the machine is there.

What is mechanical sympathy in software? A term borrowed from racing: writing code that is aware of the hardware underneath it — minimizing CPU cycles, cache-thrashing context switches, and unnecessary allocation. It is the discipline behind KDE 3.5 fitting an entire desktop in ~200MB.

Does software bloat cause electronic waste? Yes. When a laptop that ran fine in 2018 gets labeled "too slow" because the software forgot how to be small, it gets thrown out — making software bloat one of the quiet engines of e-waste.

#Linux #Xubuntu #KDE #LinuxDesktop #SoftwareBloat

title: The GPU Myth: State of AI Compute 2026 | Stephen Balaban
uploader: The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck
description:
Many people said GPU compute would become a commodity. The opposite happened — and a new category of "neoclouds" is now racing to build the physical backbone of the AI boom. Stephen Balaban, co-founder and CTO of Lambda, explains why the conventional wisdom was exactly wrong, why we're still massively underbuilding compute, and what it actually takes to stand up a gigawatt-scale AI factory: land, power, cooling, networking, and a financing stack most people have never heard of. We go deep on the physics of how energy becomes tokens, NVIDIA's real moat, why a 2023 GPU can lease for more today than the day it shipped, and Stephen's provocative vision of "neural software." Plus the wild Lambda origin story — from a facial recognition startup to a camera in a baseball cap to a near-billion-dollar cloud business. This is the state of AI compute in 2026, from inside one of the companies building it.

title: I'm Building a Fully Open Source Distributed LLM Research System
description:
Write a paper with me 1 on 1 - https://airesearchmastery.com/

llm - https://github.com/vukrosic/universe-lm

Discord (Open Superintelligence Lab) - https://discord.gg/6AbXGpKTwN

I think the best large language model in the world should be fully open source — open code, open data, open weights. In this video I walk through the system I'm building toward that: voidbase, a shared database of experiments anyone can pull and run on their own GPUs; voidspark, a local client for autonomous research; and universe LM, the model and research projects themselves.

I also talk about how I'm actually using my time — letting Opus build the massive codebase in the background while I focus on the real problem: organizing people to do AI research together, with clear tasks and short deadlines (first one up: residual attention). The long-term goal is one open, contributor-driven path to the best LLM, with the option of a centralized training cluster later.

Want to learn how to do AI research, or write a paper one-on-one? That's what funds the training runs:

Become AI Researcher (Skool) - https://www.skool.com/become-ai-researcher-2669/about
- 7+ hours of from-scratch video courses - math fundamentals, PyTorch, neural networks, transformers, reinforcement learning, LLMs
- Every lesson is code-first: you build the thing, not just watch it

title: Maximiliano Firtman - WebMCP: Making Web Apps Faster and Cheaper for Coding Agents - AI DevCon
description:
When interacting with web apps, most coding agents still rely on screenshots, DOM scraping, selector guessing, and other fragile heuristics that consume too many tokens and often break at scale.

This session explores how WebMCP can improve that workflow by giving agents a more explicit way to understand and use web apps. Instead of reverse-engineering the interface or relying on screenshots, agents can discover actions and capabilities through a structured contract exposed by the page.

We'll examine how this fits into today's MCP ecosystem, including browser automation and debugging with tools such as Chrome DevTools MCP, and look at practical workflows for testing, QA, and automated documentation. The talk also covers current limitations, security considerations, and what remains experimental in this emerging space.

Attendees leave with a realistic mental model for how coding agents interact with web apps today, what WebMCP changes, and how to start experimenting with more reliable and efficient agent-driven workflows.

title: Tutorial 5: CNNs and Transfer Learning
description:
Part 5 of 5 in the series "Image Analysis Essentials using Python."

This is where everything comes together. We show that a convolutional layer is mathematically identical to the filters we built in Tutorial 2, except the values in the kernel are learned from data rather than chosen by hand. That single insight connects every tutorial in the series.

We build a CNN from scratch that takes raw pixels as input, train it with data augmentation using tf.data, and visualize the learned filters alongside the Sobel and Gabor filters from Tutorial 2. We also use Grad-CAM to show which regions of the image drove each prediction. The scratch CNN reaches around 75% accuracy, which is meaningful improvement over the hand-crafted approach, but also shows a clear overfitting ceiling.

Then we load VGG16 pre-trained on ImageNet, freeze its convolutional weights, attach a small classification head, and fine-tune. VGG16 brings 13 layers of features learned from 1.4 million images. On our dataset it reaches 90% or higher in a fraction of the training time. The final comparison table shows all four approaches side by side: Random Forest, MLP, CNN from scratch, and VGG16 transfer learning, with accuracy, AUC, and confusion matrices for each.

Topics covered: convolutional layers, learned filters, feature maps, data augmentation, Grad-CAM, transfer learning, VGG16 architecture, fine-tuning, optimizer comparison, gradient flow, full series comparison.

Code: https://github.com/bnsreenu/python_for_microscopists/blob/master/image_analysis_essentials_V2_0.ipynb

title: LLM Interview Series #3: What Is the Transformer Architecture?
description:
Preparing for AI, ML, or LLM infrastructure interviews? 

Practice real interview-style questions here:
https://interview.vizuara.ai/

================================================================================

“What is the transformer architecture?” is one of the most important interview questions in modern AI.

Most candidates answer it at the surface level: attention, embeddings, feed-forward layers, done.

But a strong interview answer needs multiple levels of depth.

================================================================================
In this video, we first build a bird’s-eye view of how an LLM is structured, and then break down the eight core building blocks of the transformer architecture:


(1) Tokens and embeddings
(2) Positional information
(3) Attention
(4) MLP / feed-forward layers
(5) Normalization
(6) Residual connections
(7) Stacking transformer blocks
(8) Output logits

We also talk about how to deliver this answer in an interview: start with the big picture, go deeper layer by layer, and show genuine curiosity and passion for why this architecture changed AI.

The goal is not to recite a memorized diagram. The goal is to lead the discussion with clarity, depth, and first-principles thinking.

title: Message Passing: How Graph Neural Networks Actually Work
description:
Graph neural networks, explained from the ground up.

A lot of the data we care about isn't a neat table of independent rows — it's a network. People connected by friendships, atoms connected by bonds, accounts connected by transactions. In all of these, how things connect carries as much information as the things themselves.

This video builds up the core idea behind graph neural networks: how a node learns from its neighbors by passing messages, aggregating them, and updating itself — and how stacking that simple step lets information flow across an entire graph. We start from plain intuition, then make it precise with the single-layer update equation, without skipping the "why."

  What's covered:
  - Why so much real-world data is naturally a graph
  - Nodes, edges, and why the structure itself is a signal
  - Message passing: aggregate and update, one neighbor at a time
  - How layers expand a node's view to k hops away
  - The graph neural network layer equation, piece by piece

If this helped, consider subscribing for more science and technology videos. Thanks for watching.

#GraphNeuralNetworks #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #GNN #AI

title: Build the hunger, not the skill: AI Mental Model #3
description:
==============================================================
📓 Free visual lecture notes for this episode:
https://vizuaraai.github.io/great-mental-models-of-ai/lecture-03-byproduct-theorem.html
==============================================================

What if the best way to build intelligence is not to teach the skill directly?

This is Lecture 3 of The Great Mental Models of Artificial Intelligence. In this episode, we explore the Byproduct Theorem: design the right hunger, or loss function, and surprising capabilities can emerge as byproducts.

We begin with a simple parable: give a man a fish, teach him to fish, or create the hunger that forces him to invent fishing himself. That third move is the heart of modern AI.

In this lecture, we look at how the same idea appears again and again:

==============================================================
(1) Next-token prediction: predict one word, get grammar, meaning, and language as byproducts
(2) Word2Vec: predict neighboring words, get meaning as geometry
(3) Ridge regression: punish large weights, get generalization
(4) DeepSeek R1: reward correct answers, get reasoning and self-correction

title: The Caching Problem Nobody Talks About with AI Agents
description:
Link to BetterDB → https://betterdb.com/b/cl6bU 

Most of us learned caching to take load off a database. One quick lookup instead of a query. With AI agents that whole model breaks, because every cache miss is a paid call to a language model. Real tokens, real money, and a user waiting seconds instead of milliseconds.

In this video we cache every place an agent repeats work. The model calls, the tool calls, and the session reads. We handle both kinds of repeat: the exact same request, and the same question asked in different words. All of it runs on a single Valkey instance with no special modules, and by the end the cache tunes itself.

Here is what we cover:
Why caching changes completely once you put an agent in production
The three places an agent quietly repeats work
Exact repeats vs similar repeats, and why one is easy and one is hard
Valkey vs Redis, and why it matters for managed services
Agent Cache: caching the model, the tools, and the session in one line

title: Your Apps Aren't Programs. They're Web Browsers with the Address Bar Cut Off
description:
A chat window doing nothing can quietly hold 2GB of your RAM. Not a bug — the architecture. Here's the ghost living inside your desktop apps.

Most "modern" desktop apps aren't programs anymore. They're entire web browsers with the address bar sawed off. Slack, Discord, VS Code, your password manager — many ship a full copy of Chromium plus a Node.js runtime, just to render a text box. This is the Electron trade: we optimized for developer velocity and quietly agreed to pay for it with the RAM of every machine that runs the software. This video opens one of those apps up, shows the colony of browser processes hiding inside, and explains why 16GB went from a power-user flex to the bare minimum — and what local-first, natively-compiled tools like Tauri are doing to claw the silicon back.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
If you want the root of all of this — why hardware 10,000 times faster than a generation ago still leaves you waiting — watch the companion piece: "10,000x More Power, Still Slow — Here's Why." Think of that one as the theory. This is what the theory looks like running on your machine.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 4KB to the Moon, 2GB for a Text Box
1:01 Every App Is Secretly a Browser
1:53 The Architecture of Laziness
2:32 Is This Normal? Anatomy of the Bloat
3:59 Why 10,000x Faster Still Feels Slow
4:16 The Death of Mechanical Sympathy
5:15 2026: When 16GB Became the Floor
6:01 Reclaiming the Silicon (Tauri, Local-First)
7:02 You're Not Haunted. You're Feeding It.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Electron — the framework that ships a desktop app as a bundled web browser
- Chromium — the browser engine (same one inside Google Chrome) copied into each Electron app
- V8 — the JavaScript engine that compiles your code on the fly inside that browser
- Node.js — the JavaScript runtime bundled alongside Chromium in every Electron app
- Tauri — a native/local-first toolkit that reuses the OS rendering engine instead of bundling Chromium
- Mechanical sympathy — writing code that respects the real, finite hardware underneath it
- Local-first — software designed to run on the machine, in the machine's own language
- AGC (Apollo Guidance Computer) — the 4KB-working-memory machine that flew to the Moon in 1969

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why do desktop apps use so much RAM now? Many are built with Electron, which bundles a full copy of the Chromium browser engine and a Node.js runtime into each app — so launching one app effectively launches a whole web browser to render its interface.

Is it normal for apps to use 13GB of RAM while idle? Yes. With several Electron apps open (chat, code editor, notes), each running its own browser engine, idle usage in the 10-13GB range on a 16GB machine is now common — which is exactly the problem this video is about.

Why does opening 5 apps feel like running 5 browsers? Because it literally is. Each Electron app ships and runs its own separate copy of Chromium, none of them aware the others exist, so the browser overhead multiplies per app.

What's the alternative to Electron? Natively-compiled, local-first toolkits like Tauri reuse the rendering engine the operating system already provides and put a compiled native core (Rust/C++) underneath, often dropping idle memory from hundreds of MB to a few tens of MB.

Did Discord's memory fixes solve it? Discord patched these leaks across nine separate fixes since October 2025, dropping peak usage about 5% — but the app still holds hundreds of MB doing nothing, because the cost is the architecture, not a single bug.

Why is the Apollo / 4KB comparison relevant? In 1969 a computer with 4KB of working memory flew three men to the Moon and back; 2GB — roughly 500,000 times that — is now spent just to render a line of text, which captures how much capability we trade for convenience.

#Electron #RAM #SoftwareEngineering #Chromium #Tauri

title: The Creators of Claude Code and OpenClaw don't Prompt Their Agents Anymore?!
description:
The best AI engineers barely prompt their agents anymore. Sounds crazy I know! They build a loop that does the prompting for them and let it run for hours while they step away. The head of Claude Code at Anthropic put it bluntly: he doesn't prompt Claude, he has loops running that prompt Claude for him.

The only problem is this gets expensive (and hallucinations compound) - you need a harness with observability that can run reliably not always using the expensive frontier models.

So I built one! It's a TypeScript app that runs Pi (on Kimi) in a loop, stores every run in a database, and shows it all in a dashboard. And the loop isn't one agent on repeat, it's agents prompting agents: an orchestrator decides the next tasks and spins up workers to run them in parallel. Think of it like an advanced Ralph loop with more control!

title: Office Is Almost Ready...
Banks Know the System Is Fragile. They Just Can't Do Anything About It
description:
The financial world runs on legacy code so old it predates the moon landing — and the engineers who understand it are retiring faster than anyone can replace them. This is the systemic software fragility nobody at the top wants to talk about.

Pensions, payrolls, roughly 95% of ATM swipes, around $3 trillion in commerce a day — much of it still passes through systems written in the 1960s. The code isn't broken. It's the most reliable thing in the building. The danger is quieter: the people who know how to read it are aging out, the schools stopped teaching it, and replacing it has gone wrong badly enough that almost everyone is too afraid to try. This is a story about legacy banking systems, the COBOL maintainer crisis, and the fear of migrating infrastructure that can never be turned off.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
If the idea of standing on code nobody understands unsettled you, the next freeze is one we're building right now — on purpose, at scale.
▶ The AI Code Debt Bomb is About to Explode

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Scoreboard
0:47 The Stadium You Never See
2:13 The Relief That Never Comes
4:35 The Cost of Fear
7:06 2 A.M.
8:31 What Comes Next

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Legacy code — decades-old software still running critical financial systems in production
- COBOL maintainer crisis — aging programmers retiring with no one trained to replace them
- Systemic software fragility — when reliability breeds neglect and knowledge concentrates in one person's head
- Bus factor / key-person risk — undocumented knowledge that lives in a single retiring engineer
- The migration trap — why a system that can never go offline can't be safely rewritten
- The 2018 UK bank migration — ~1.9M of 5.2M customers locked out; ~£330M cleanup; ~£48.6M regulator fine
- The 2020 New Jersey unemployment surge — ~362,000 claims in ~2 weeks; a public plea for old-language volunteers
- Open-heart-surgery problem — replacing infrastructure on a live patient that can't be put to sleep

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why don't banks just replace old legacy code? Replacing it is dangerous and expensive — these systems can never go offline, and past migrations (like a 2018 UK bank) locked out millions of customers and cost hundreds of millions, so most institutions choose to keep paying to maintain the old system instead.

What happens when the programmers who maintain this code retire? The knowledge often lives in one person's head and was never documented, so when they retire the institution loses the only person who understands why the system behaves the way it does — and the schools mostly stopped teaching the language.

Is the old 1968-era code actually broken? No. It's one of the most reliable things in the building and has kept accurate records for half a century. The risk isn't the code failing — it's that almost nobody left understands what it was told to do.

How much of the financial system still runs on legacy code? By widely cited estimates, hundreds of billions of lines remain in production, roughly 95% of ATM transactions touch these systems, and around $3 trillion in commerce moves across them every day.

Is new AI-generated code safer than this old code? Not necessarily — the video argues the opposite. Code generated fast by tools that don't fully understand it may become tomorrow's emergency, which is the subject of the related video, The AI Code Debt Bomb is About to Explode.

#legacycode #COBOL #softwareengineering #fintech #systemrisk

title: 🎥 Statistics 4.3 – Multiplication Rules for Probability | Ind./Dep. Events & Conditional Probability
description:
Welcome to Chapter 4.3: Multiplication Rules for Probability! In this lesson, we explore how to calculate the probability of events happening in sequence. Whether you’re drawing cards, selecting committee members, or assigning class sections, multiplication rules help you determine the likelihood of multiple events occurring together.

We’ll cover:
✅ Independent events — what they are and how to recognize them
— Experiments with replacement  
— The multiplication rule:
𝑃(𝐸 and 𝐹)=𝑃(𝐸)⋅𝑃(𝐹)
  
— Examples with cards, workplace infractions, and more
✅ Dependent events — when one event affects the probability of the next
— Experiments without replacement  
— How probabilities change after each stage
— Step‑by‑step examples: drawing cards, selecting club members, and real‑world scenarios
✅ Conditional probability  
— What it means to calculate 
𝑃(𝐹∣𝐸)  
— How to interpret “given that” in context
— Examples using job applicants and graduate degrees
— Why conditional probability is essential for dependent events
✅ The Multiplication Rule for Dependent Events  
𝑃(𝐸 and 𝐹)=𝑃(𝐸)⋅𝑃(𝐹∣𝐸)
— How to apply it correctly
— How it connects to conditional probability
✅ The Fundamental Counting Principle  
— How to count outcomes in multistage experiments
— With and without replacement
— Examples involving course schedules, board elections, and snack choices
— How counting connects to probability
✅ Spreadsheet setup for calculating multiplication‑rule probabilities with proper formatting

You’ll see how to break down multistage experiments, how to distinguish between independent and dependent events, and how to compute probabilities using both the multiplication rule and conditional probability. By the end of the video, you’ll be able to analyze sequences of events with confidence.

🎓 Perfect for:  
Introductory Statistics students
Business, social science, health, or STEM majors
Anyone learning how to calculate probabilities for events that occur in sequence

title: AI Still Sees Like a Toddler. Andrew Dai Wants to Fix It
description:
AI can write code, pass exams, and summarize the internet.
But can it actually understand what it sees?

In this episode of The Neuron, Corey Noles and Grant Harvey sit down with Andrew Dai, co-founder and CEO of Elorian, to talk about one of the biggest unsolved problems in AI: visual reasoning.

Andrew spent years at Google Brain and DeepMind, including work connected to Gemini and sparse mixture-of-experts systems. Now, he’s building Elorian to tackle the gap between image recognition and real visual understanding.

We get into why today’s frontier models can identify objects but still struggle with tasks that kids can solve, why language-based reasoning breaks down when images get too complex, and what it would take for AI to reason through diagrams, designs, tangled cords, charts, floor plans, robots, and the physical world.

In this conversation:

• Why visual reasoning is more than image description
• The difference between pattern matching and true reasoning
• Why scaling language models hasn’t solved vision
• What “visual chain of thought” could mean
• Why better visual AI could change engineering, robotics, satellite analysis, and product design
• How Elorian is approaching specialist models for visual problems

title: GenAI is Changing the Future of Development Teams
description:
Is your development team losing weeks to non-differentiating work? In this episode, Steve Smith explores how Generative AI tools like Cursor, Claude, and GitHub Copilot are fundamentally shifting the economics of software delivery.

While much of the conversation around GenAI focuses on coding speed, the true transformation lies in how these tools can compress entire delivery lifecycles—turning tasks that once took days or weeks into mere minutes.

In this video, we discuss:
- The GenAI Shift: Why coding 10x faster is only half the battle if your downstream processes remain manual.
- Reclaiming Developer Capacity: How AI can handle the "salt mines" of non-differentiating work, such as patching security vulnerabilities and library upgrades, to free teams for high-value features.
- Modernising the Machine: Why organisations must rethink assurance activities, like end-to-end testing and Change Approval Boards (CABs), to keep pace with AI-driven development.
- The Future of Quality: Using AI to lower knowledge barriers and automate functional testing, allowing testers to focus on high-impact exploratory work.
- Organisational Guardrails: Navigating the procurement, security, and technical foundational work required to safely deploy GenAI at scale.

title: 🔬 The Limits of AI in Science - Why We Need Self-Driving Labs — Joseph Krause, Radical AI
description:
Joseph Krause, founder and CEO of Radical AI, joins the AI for Science podcast to make the case that the bottleneck holding back aerospace, defense, computing, and consumer products isn't ideas — it's experiments. His answer is the self-driving lab: a closed-loop system where an AI scientist generates hypotheses and a fully automated lab synthesizes, characterizes, and tests them at speeds no human team can match. In six months, Radical has produced 1,200 alloys — nearly 10x the pace of the best prior DARPA program — with 300 novel compositions and 10 already being developed for commercial applications. We dig into why no model can one-shot a material, how their AI scientist is exploring elemental families human scientists never considered, the geopolitical race with China on materials, and why Joseph believes the moat in this industry is the lab and the data, not the model.

title: 3 philosophy classics that are better than self-help books
description:
The most common question I get asked is what books I'd recommend. The second most common is: what's the point of philosophy? This video answers both.

I've always seen philosophy as a kind of therapy — at its best when it helps us live our lives, understand our relationships, and make better decisions. Most self-help is really just philosophy in a new set of clothes: Aristotle in podcast form, or Lao Tzu giving a TED talk. The problem is that every time you simplify a big idea, you lose a little nuance. Simplify too many times and it becomes worthless.

So here are three books where philosophy is richest at its source — the real thing, straight from the well.

The Books:

Nicomachean Ethics — Aristotle's genius guidebook on how to be happy, how to be virtuous, and how to be human. 

Essays — Michel de Montaigne, the father of the essay, talking through his solutions to problems just as relevant today as they were 400 years ago.

Dao de Jing — Lao Tzu's collection of aphorisms that refuse to give you straight answers — and make you wonder if there are any to be found.

Sometimes the best thing about philosophy isn't the ideas themselves, but the process of considering them.

title: Turn Any LLM Into an Expert 📚 RAG Coding Crash Course
description:
What if you could take any language model and turn it into an expert by giving it fast access to documents? 🤔 Big thanks to HubSpot for sponsoring this video 🙌
⭐ Check out HubSpot's FREE AI Agents Cheat Sheet:
https://clickhubspot.com/0acb23

In this beginner-friendly RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) project, we will build a local AI system that can read custom documents, search through them intelligently, and answer questions using knowledge that was never part of the original model! 📚⚡

To make things fun, we'll use a collection of fictional investigation files inspired by The Lord Of The Rings and The Matrix — turning an ordinary LLM into a specialized AI detective capable of solving mysteries hidden throughout our documents: analyzing suspicious characters, secret reports, and hidden clues like a real pro! 🧙‍♂️🕶️

Unlike Fine Tuning, we won't train the model at all! Instead, we will allow a tiny and not-so-smart model to search through documents very quickly, retrieve the most relevant information, and use it to generate intelligent answers in real time — as tough we are dealing with a giant super-smart model that requires way more resources and computing power. 😎

By the end of this tutorial, you'll have a complete local RAG application running on your own computer using Python, LangChain, Ollama and FAISS — and you'll finally understand why Fine Tuning and RAG are not the same thing!

📚 What You'll Learn:

* What RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) actually is
* Loading PDF documents into Python
* Document chunking and text splitting
* Creating embeddings from text
* Storing embeddings with FAISS
* Retrieving relevant context from a vector database
* Building a complete RAG pipeline with LangChain
* Running local LLMs with Ollama
* Answering questions using custom knowledge

🛠 Tools Used:

* Python
* Ollama
* Qwen2.5:1.5B (small enough to run on CPU)
* BGE-M3
* LangChain
* FAISS
* Jupyter Lab

🚨 Are you an educator?

Feel free to use my video slides and project idea in your classes! 🙂
I've included a high-quality PDF containing all the slides from this video, which you can download directly from YouTube.
The complete source code, notebook, and project files are also available on GitHub. You're welcome to use them in your lectures, workshops, and classroom activities.

Happy teaching! 💚

🔎 Resources & Helpful Tutorials:

⭐ Full Code and Starter Files on GitHub: https://github.com/MariyaSha/rag_ollama
⭐ My WSL and Conda Tutorial: https://youtu.be/luM5kwH6tjQ

⭐ Classroom Slides (PDF): Download directly from YouTube 📄
⭐ Ollama and FAISS GPU links in the pinned comment 📌

conda activate rag_env

pip install \

title: LM Studio: Easiest Way to Get Started with Local Models (Even on Your Phone)
description:
Running a local AI model doesn't have to mean the terminal — LM Studio makes it a one-click download, and its new LM Link feature puts that model on your phone, fully private.

Sign up for my FREE weekly newsletter, where I spill my unfiltered thoughts on the latest AI news, cool research, and projects I'm building: https://www.onchainaigarage.com/

🐦 Follow Tonbi on X for real-time AI x blockchain updates! https://x.com/tonbistudio

The friendliest way to run a local AI model — no terminal, no CLI, no idea what a GGUF file is required. After my Ollama and llama.cpp videos, this one is for everyone else: I install LM Studio, let it pick a model for my hardware (Gemma 4), and start chatting in a couple of minutes, then tour the parts that matter — model settings and GPU offload in a clean UI, the model browser that tells you whether a model fits your GPU, the local server with OpenAI- and Anthropic-compatible endpoints, and a quick honest comparison with Ollama (same llama.cpp/CUDA backend underneath, but LM Studio gives you a full GUI and is proprietary, while Ollama is MIT open source). The highlight is LM Link (new as of June 4th, free in preview): connecting the full-size model running on my PC to my phone, end-to-end encrypted. I show the setup with the "Locally" app and then dig into whether it's actually private — it's built on Tailscale's WireGuard mesh networking, so your phone and PC form a direct encrypted tunnel and the only thing touching LM Studio's servers is a tiny device directory so the two can find each other. I also cover how this differs from Apple Intelligence (tiny on-device models) — LM Link is really remote access to your own home GPU, so the one tradeoff is your desktop has to stay awake.

Resources:
🔗 LM Studio: https://lmstudio.ai/

Timestamps:
0:00 - Why local models (privacy) + what LM Studio is
2:29 - Installing LM Studio + your first model
4:13 - Chatting + model settings the easy way
6:42 - Finding and downloading models
7:46 - The developer tab + local server endpoints
8:43 - LM Studio vs Ollama
9:52 - LM Link: running your model on your phone
13:48 - How LM Link works (is it actually private?)
14:50 - LM Link vs Apple Intelligence

Are you running local models yet — and would using yours from your phone change how often you reach for them? Let me know in the comments. I'll keep covering the most approachable ways to run AI locally, so if this helped please like, subscribe, and hit the bell! 🦐✨

title: Which IT Career Identity Will Survive the Next 10 Years?
description:
In every mythical story there's one character who stays immortal — watching generations come and go, never defeated. The IT industry has its own version of this.

Whether you're watching this in 2026 or in 2036, these 5 identities will always thrive in the IT industry. AI can replace your tasks. AI can replace your roles. But AI cannot replace your IDENTITY — and that's the one thing that survives every wave.

In this video I break down the 5 immortal identities every IT professional should build — the ADTSS framework. The first three are technical. The last two are human-centric. Most of us carry a percentage of all five — your job is to find which one resonates and build on it.

Whether you're a mid-career professional aiming for SRE, DevOps, architecture, pre-sales, or leadership roles, this video will help you reflect on where you're strong and where you need to grow.

📌 Your homework: reflect on which identity aligns with you most — and which one you're weakest in.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS
00:00 Introduction

title: Your Agent's Biggest Lie: "I Searched the Web" — Rafael Levi, Bright Data
description:
Sometimes the agent did not search the web at all. It got blocked, hit a CAPTCHA, saw a fake page, or fell back to stale training data, then answered as if everything worked. This session is a direct look at that failure mode, and what changes when the same agent is given real web access instead of pretending.

Using Bright Data's Web MCP, the demo compares blocked and unblocked runs across sites like LinkedIn, Instagram, Amazon, and TikTok, and walks through the mechanics behind the difference: anti-bot systems, JS rendering, CAPTCHA handling, and why clean access matters if you want reliable citations, real-time results, and fewer hallucinations. If you're building agents that depend on the open web, this is a practical look at one of their biggest hidden failure modes.

Speaker info:
- https://il.linkedin.com/in/rafael-levi
- https://github.com/ScrapeAlchemist

Charles Duhigg, author of Super Communicators, spent years studying why smart, well-intentioned people consistently fail to connect.

title: Choose Boredom Over the Next Framework Hype
description:
The frontend framework you're learning right now has an expiration date — roughly 36 months — and almost nobody plans for it. This is why frameworks die so fast, and what to build on instead.

Every cycle we're sold the same promise: more abstraction means better software. It doesn't. It means another burial. State managers (Flux, Redux, MobX, Recoil, Zustand, Jotai), build tools (Grunt, Gulp, Webpack, Rollup, Parcel, Vite), and entire frameworks get declared "the future," then quietly demoted to legacy within half a decade — while the apps built on them freeze into architectural debt. This deep-dive unpacks resume-driven development, the difference between official and *cultural* deprecation, what a virtual DOM actually costs, and why the escape isn't another framework — it's a return to web platform primitives.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO ON THE CHANNEL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This essay ends where the next one begins. If frameworks die but the code outlives them, what happens when that legacy software runs on hardware thousands of times faster — and still feels slow? Watch: "10,000x More Power, Still Slow — Here's Why."

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Silicon Cemetery
0:47 The Great Tech Burnout
1:54 Frozen Apps and the Debt Bubble
2:35 The Ergonomics Trap
3:30 The Three-Year Death Spiral
5:35 The Cost of Losing Primitives
6:54 Escape From the Cemetery
9:08 Which Timeline Are You Optimizing For?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS & TOOLS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- State managers: Flux, Redux, MobX, Recoil, Zustand, Jotai
- Build tools: Grunt, Gulp, Webpack, Rollup, Parcel, Vite
- Frameworks: AngularJS (EOL 2021), Angular 2, React (class components → hooks), Vue 2 → Vue 3 (Composition API)
- Virtual DOM — runtime diffing layer and what it actually costs
- Web Components & standard browser APIs (the "primitives")
- TC39 Signals — Stage 1 proposal to add reactivity as a native JS primitive (backed by Angular, Vue, Solid, Svelte, MobX)
- Vue 3.6 Vapor Mode — compiles components to direct DOM operations, no virtual DOM
- Concepts: resume-driven development, architectural debt, official vs cultural deprecation, "choose boredom on purpose"

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why do frontend frameworks die so fast? Core contributors get famous, the original problem gets solved, they leave, and maintainers who inherited the codebase issue a "rewrite or die" ultimatum — roughly every 36 months.

What is cultural deprecation? When the community, docs, tutorials, and job postings move on from your knowledge even though the code was never officially deprecated. React class components still work, but hooks culturally replaced them.

Should I stop learning React or Vue? No — keep frameworks at the edges where they're cheap to replace, and build the long-lived core of a system on web platform primitives (Web Components, standard APIs) that don't expire.

Is the virtual DOM going away? The frameworks themselves are moving past it: Vue 3.6's Vapor Mode compiles to direct DOM operations, and TC39 Signals would add native reactivity — both admit the abstraction was the cost.

#webdev #javascript #frontend #webdevelopment #ProgrammingDeepDive

title: Recursive language models, explained (the idea under Claude "ultracode")
description:
On May 28th, 2026, Anthropic shipped "dynamic workflows" in Claude Code, switched on by a setting that sounds like a cheat code: ultracode. Flip it, hand the AI a big job, and it does something strange — it stops trying to answer you. Instead it writes a program, and that program spins up an army of copies of the AI: fifty, sometimes a few hundred, each on its own slice of the work, while the one in charge runs the whole operation from code and never even holds most of what comes back.

People can't agree whether it's the biggest leap in a year or an expensive way to burn money — but that argument isn't the interesting part. This video is about how it pulls the trick off at all: the MIT idea underneath it, recursive language models — stop feeding the model your data, leave it in a variable, and make it write code to reach in for only the scraps it needs. We walk through why one overloaded context window "rots," the company-org-chart shape that fixes it (recursion is just a manager who's allowed to hire), the benchmark where the same models jump from basically zero to 76, why everyone's so careful with it (burned budgets, agents that hallucinate and then confirm each other), how one lab trained the skill into the model instead of prompting it, and when this is actually worth using. It ends on the shift it forces: you stop asking "how do I solve this?" and start asking "how would I structure a team to solve it?" If you want more under-the-hood breakdowns like this, subscribe — and tell me in the comments where you'd point an AI army first.

## Chapters
00:00 One AI writes a program that spawns an army of itself
00:50 "Isn't this just subagents?" — the one real difference
02:05 Recursive language models: stop feeding the model your data
02:58 Why one overloaded context rots — and the fix
05:01 The proof: same models, 0 → 76
06:18 It's everywhere — and why everyone's careful
07:43 Teaching the skill, and when to actually use it
09:12 The bigger shift: you draw the org chart

## Sources & further reading
- Anthropic — Dynamic Workflows in Claude Code (announcement) — https://claude.com/blog/introducing-dynamic-workflows-in-claude-code
- Anthropic — A harness for every task: Dynamic Workflows — https://claude.com/blog/a-harness-for-every-task-dynamic-workflows-in-claude-code
- Recursive Language Models — Alex Zhang's blog (with interactive trajectories) — https://alexzhang13.github.io/blog/2025/rlm/
- Recursive Language Models (Zhang, Kraska, Khattab — MIT CSAIL), arXiv — https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24601
- CodeWhale — the DeepSeek coding agent that wired RLM in, in the open — https://github.com/Hmbown/CodeWhale
- Kimi agent swarm + PARL (Moonshot), arXiv — https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02276

title: Mastra vs AI SDK: I Built Production Apps With Both
description:
Everyone keeps asking the same question: "Should I use Mastra or AI SDK?" After building production AI applications with both, I think that's the wrong question to be asking.
In this video, I break down why Mastra and AI SDK aren't really competitors — they operate at completely different layers. AI SDK is focused on model interaction, while Mastra is focused on application infrastructure. In fact, Mastra uses AI SDK under the hood, and even if you choose Mastra, you'll still rely on AI SDK components like the useChat hook and embedding functions.

The real questions you should be asking:
→ How much AI infrastructure does my product actually need?
→ Do I want to own and maintain that infrastructure myself?

I walk through what happens when AI products stop being simple — conversation history, memory, long-term memory, semantic recall, working memory, browser and computer use, tracing, observability, evaluations — and how you slowly stop building a feature and start building an entire AI infrastructure. Then I explain how to actually make the decision for your own project.

If you're trying to decide between Mastra and AI SDK, hopefully this gives you a clearer way to think about it.

🐦 Follow me on X: https://x.com/kristianvtr

title: AI agents can manage your passwords. Should we let them? Plus: The biggest Patch Tuesday ever.
description:
Explore the podcast →https://ibm.biz/~1RGiVfNK3

Apple unveiled an AI agent that can detect if your password’s been compromised and change it for you. 

The question is: Should you let it?

On this episode of Security Intelligence, Michelle Alvarez, Erblind Morina and Austin Zeizel join host Matt Kosinski to discuss the promise and pitfalls of using AI agents to address the pernicious issue of cybersecurity hygiene. As people, we’re not great at it. But are the agents ready for primetime?

Then, we talk about Microsoft’s biggest Patch Tuesday ever: more than 200 distinct CVEs were covered in the June 2026 release. We talk about why this might just be the beginning of a new era in vulnerability management, and why it matters. Spoiler: It’s not really about the numbers.

Finally, we get into a report that C-suite executives are increasingly willing to take on greater cyber risk in exchange for innovation. Some say it’s a reaction after years of security spending with low ROI—but are they overcorrecting?

All that and more on Security Intelligence.

description:
A multi-part coding interview with a candidate interviewing for software roles at AI labs.
