title: Why AI Looks Like Magic in Demos and Breaks in Production
description:
A demo is a retouched photo. A deployment is a city in motion. AI doesn't have an intelligence problem in production — it has an entropy problem, and the keynote never showed you that part.

This is the close of a trilogy on why AI looks unstoppable on stage and stalls the moment it touches the real world. Not because the model got dumber between the demo and your office — because the world got louder. Dirty data, contradictory context, latency you can't hide, an inference cost curve that never reaches zero, and a runtime that refuses to answer the same question the same way twice. Five places where the photo and the city stop matching — and why the next real revolution in AI won't come from a bigger model, but from deployment engineering.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, dark editorial essays on the systems behind the hype — no sponsors, reader-supported.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WATCH NEXT (FROM THIS CHANNEL)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
"Modern software is spending your attention without asking" — if this video showed you the cost of getting AI INTO the world, that one shows you the cost of the software that's already here, quietly spending something of yours without asking. Not your money. Your attention.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Anomaly
0:21 A Demo Is a Photo, a Deployment Is a City
1:17 Act I — The Mirage
2:29 Act II — The Context Wall
4:18 Act III — The Economics of Inference
6:17 Act IV — The Reliability Problem
7:58 The Infinite Prototype

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The demo-to-deployment gap (entropy, not intelligence)
- Cherry-picking and the curated demo take
- Dirty data as a "crime scene" vs. the demo's clean inputs
- RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) and why finding beats thinking
- The context window myth — bigger prompt, same contradictions
- Inference economics: latency and the marginal-cost slope that never hits zero
- "No free million" — why AI has no zero-marginal-cost user
- Determinism vs. probabilism — nondeterminism as runtime, not bug
- Temperature 0 and structured output — locking the envelope, not the letter
- The Infinite Prototype — and deployment engineering as the next moat

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why does AI work in demos but fail in production? A demo runs on clean, prepared data under perfect conditions and shows one curated take; production hands the model dirty data, contradictory context, real latency, and the same question asked thousands of times — the model didn't get dumber, the environment got louder.

Is the problem that AI isn't smart enough? No. Intelligence and operational entropy are almost unrelated problems. You can max out one and faceplant on the other — the deployment gap is about surviving mess, not about IQ.

What is RAG and why doesn't it fix everything? RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) lets a model search your documents before answering. The hard part isn't the thinking, it's the finding — when ten thousand internal docs contradict each other, retrieval just hands the model a stack of conflicting truths and it confidently picks one.

Doesn't a bigger context window solve the context problem? No — pouring all your documents into one prompt just puts more of the same contradictions in view. The model got bigger; the basement didn't get organized.

If inference prices keep dropping, isn't cost solved? Prices fell faster than almost any technology in history, but cheaper is not free. The marginal-cost curve compressed toward zero without touching it — at scale, every single answer still pays rent, every day.

Why can't you just make AI deterministic? Nondeterminism is the runtime, not a bug. Pinning temperature to zero and forcing a fixed format structures the envelope but not the letter — the reasoning inside still wanders, which breaks tests, debugging, and certification.

#AI #MachineLearning #AIengineering #LLM #TechExplained

title: Deep Autoregressive Models | Part 1 - Image Generation with PixelCNN
description:
After last time covering VAEs and how to learn data distributions, it’s time to look at an entirely different philosophy of data generation: exact likelihood tracking. 
Our first stop in this family is the Deep Autoregressive Model, specifically focusing on the foundational PixelCNN. In my opinion, tracking how a network generates images pixel by pixel is one of the most elegant and satisfying architectures to study in deep learning.

In this video, we’ll dive deep into:

What makes a generative model "autoregressive" and why ordering matters.
How Masked Convolutions explicitly enforce the causal structure so the model never "cheats."
 Why exact likelihood calculation gives us nice results, but leaves us with slow, sequential generation times.

Enjoy the video! Feel free to drop a comment with any questions—I’ll be happy to help.

title: You Don't Need React Portals For Modals Anymore (react-call)

title: Why 4-Bit AI Models Still Work (Quantization Explained)
description:
If you are reading the description, you found the hidden quantizer

Most people skip this part, so here is your technical treat:

Quantizing an LLM’s weights does not necessarily solve its biggest memory problem. During long conversations, the model stores a KV cache of previous tokens, and this cache grows with every token generated—eventually it can rival or even exceed the memory used by the weights. Researchers have therefore pushed KV-cache quantization as low as 2 bits; one method, KIVI, reported 2.6× lower peak memory and support for up to 4× larger batches while maintaining nearly the same model quality.

If you read this, comment "Debug Mode" below. Let's confuse the non-readers ;)

Music: 'Horizons' by Scott Buckley - released under CC-BY 4.0. www.scottbuckley.com.au

title: Where to use Reinforcement Learning | part 2

description:

Most people think they’re good at AI because they use ChatGPT or Claude every day. But using AI frequently doesn’t mean you’re using it effectively and in some cases, adding AI to a bad workflow can actually make you slower.

In this video, I break down the 5 levels of AI proficiency every developer should understand in 2026. From using basic chatbots to building production-ready AI systems and orchestrating autonomous agents. 

💡 Here’s what you’ll learn:
✅ Level 1: Consumer — using AI chatbots for everyday tasks, learning, writing, and debugging
✅ Level 2: Practitioner — building reusable prompts, workflows, projects, and custom instructions
✅ Level 3: Builder — creating real AI features with APIs, RAG, vector databases, and evals
✅ Level 4: Architect — designing reliable AI systems with routing, caching, fallbacks, and observability
✅ Level 5: Orchestrator — managing autonomous agents, specifications, testing loops, and AI workflows
✅ Why using AI more doesn’t always make developers faster or more productive
✅ How to move from basic prompting to building production-ready AI products

title: How a Broken Protocol Conquered the Internet
description:
Everything about the internet changed since 1982 — except the one engine that still moves your email. SMTP has no native security, trusts anyone who says hello, and carries nearly half its traffic as spam. By every rule of engineering it should have collapsed. It didn't.

This is the story of why a 1982 protocol with a fatal design flaw became impossible to kill — and the uncomfortable price we paid to keep it alive. From the ARPANET club where everyone trusted everyone, to the open-relay siege of the 90s, to the infinite patches (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) bolted onto a handshake that was never designed to ask who you are. And the irony hiding underneath all of it: to save a decentralized protocol from spam, we quietly handed the keys to a handful of companies in a handful of buildings.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
If these breakdowns of the systems running underneath modern life are worth something to you, the channel runs on viewer support.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RELATED VIDEO
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SMTP earned its place — it survived because it was simple. But not every piece of software you touch every day aged that gracefully. Some of it is failing under its own weight and quietly taking something from you that you can't get back.
▶ Watch next: "Modern software is spending your attention without asking"

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The Improbable Survivor
0:10 SMTP and the Handshake That Trusts You
1:08 1982: An Age of Trust
2:22 The Siege: Open Relays and Spam
4:05 Why You Can't Just Build SMTP 2.0
5:16 The Infinite Patches: SPF, DKIM, DMARC
7:55 The Cost of Survival: Centralization
9:39 Good Enough Beats Perfect

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) — RFC 821, August 1982, Jon Postel
- Open relay — the default that turned a flaw into a spammer's megaphone
- Network effect — why a better protocol nobody speaks is worthless
- SPF (Sender Policy Framework) — the guest list
- DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) — the tamper-proof wax seal
- DMARC — the policy that finally says what to do when checks fail
- ESMTP / STARTTLS / SMTPUTF8 — backward-compatible extensions (EHLO falls back to HELO)
- Google + Yahoo bulk sender requirements (February 2024)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FAQ
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why does email still run on a protocol from 1982? Because SMTP spread so far so fast that it stopped being technology and became infrastructure — replacing it would require every mail server on Earth to switch at once, which the network effect makes impossible.

Why couldn't spam kill SMTP? Because the fix was never replacing the protocol. Administrators closed open relays server by server, then wrapped SMTP in external layers (SPF, DKIM, DMARC). Good enough and everywhere beat perfect and nowhere.

Does SMTP have any built-in security? No. The original protocol just believes whoever says hello — no password, no proof of sender. All modern email security lives outside SMTP, in DNS and headers.

What are SPF, DKIM, and DMARC? Three external patches that try to prove sender identity in a protocol built to never ask: SPF is a published list of allowed servers, DKIM is a cryptographic signature, and DMARC is the policy for what to do when those checks fail.

Why is email controlled by Google and Microsoft now? Running a mail server the world trusts means configuring SPF, DKIM, and DMARC flawlessly and guarding your reputation full-time. Few can, so a handful of giant providers now send, route, and judge most of the world's email — centralizing a protocol that was designed to be decentralized.

#SMTP #emailsecurity #networking #internethistory #protocols

title: CSES Speedrun - Graphs - Part III
description:
This is part 3 of me doing problems from the CSES problem set dealing with graphs. Here we get into the harder shortest-path and cycle territory: Bellman-Ford for both positive and negative cycle detection, Dijkstra extended with extra state (DP on Dijkstra), K shortest paths, and directed cycle detection with 3-coloring DFS, with parent-pointer path reconstruction throughout.

title: PostgreSQL Can Replace Your Entire Stack
description:
PostgreSQL can replace your document store, search engine, vector database, maps engine, and job queue - without running a single extra service.

In this video, we go from a tangled five-service architecture down to one engine, with the actual queries: JSONB for documents, full-text search instead of Elasticsearch, pgvector for embeddings and RAG, PostGIS for geospatial, and SKIP LOCKED for a safe work queue.

Then we go under the hood. You'll learn the SQL most people half-use (window functions, CTEs, upsert, RETURNING), how to debug slow queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE and pick the right index type (B-tree, GIN, GiST, BRIN), how MVCC lets readers and writers slide past each other without blocking, why VACUUM exists, how the write-ahead log survives crashes and powers replication, and the connection gotcha that brings servers to their knees - plus the fix (PgBouncer). And when NOT to use Postgres.


More Videos -
Databases - https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLWP-VtjCVpWyNvaO0Jtbo8_hrLK7bygob
Software Egineering Basics - https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLWP-VtjCVpWyLNBm3zz_sGyC5mVwiAOvj

title: Your Favorite App Was Doomed Before You Installed It
description:
You remember when this app was good — it opened instantly, showed you what you came for, and asked nothing in return. Then it slowly turned hostile. This isn't bad luck or bad CEOs. The decline was written into the math of venture capital before you ever installed it.

This is the close of the trilogy: a breakdown of why good software has an expiration date — the power law, the subsidy phase, and the enshittification curve that forces almost every app you love to get worse on a schedule.

☕ Support Macro Lens on Patreon → https://www.patreon.com/c/MacroLens
Independent, ad-light deep-dives. Patreon keeps them that way.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WATCH NEXT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This same grow-forever logic is now being poured into one sector at a scale we've never seen. See where it goes when you multiply it by a trillion dollars:
▶ AI is a Financial Black Hole

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHAPTERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
0:00 The app that used to be good
1:14 The math that built the trap
3:10 The subsidy: the Trojan horse
5:11 Enshittification, explained
6:38 The apps you actually use
8:56 The way out: Small Tech
10:34 Where this goes next

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CONCEPTS MENTIONED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Power law — why ~4% of venture deals carry the entire fund, and ~65% lose money (per Correlation Ventures, 21,000+ deals)
- The subsidy phase — burning venture money to make a product unsustainably good and capture the market
- Enshittification — Cory Doctorow's term (2022), American Dialect Society Word of the Year 2023, for the three-stage platform decay
- Value extraction — value flows to users, then to business customers, then to shareholders
- The cap table — where the expiration date is really printed: the term sheet
- Small Tech — building profitable, sustainable, "finished" software with a price on the homepage

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FREQUENTLY ASKED
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Why do apps get worse over time? Because the venture-capital model needs exponential growth, not a sustainable business. Once a company locks you in, the only way the math closes is to extract value from you — so the product is engineered to get worse for users and better for shareholders.

What is enshittification? A term coined by writer Cory Doctorow in 2022 for a three-stage platform life cycle: first the platform is good to users, then it favors its business customers, then it claws value back for its shareholders. It was named the American Dialect Society's Word of the Year in 2023.

Why does venture capital make software worse? Venture funds run on a power law — roughly 4% of deals return the fund while about 65% lose money — so a merely profitable business counts as a failure. That pressure forces apps toward infinite growth, which eventually means extracting from the very users it once delighted.

Is there any way out? Yes — "Small Tech": software built to be a good, finished business with an honest price on the homepage, instead of a venture-backed product where the real price is paid later in your attention, data, and degrading experience.

#enshittification #venturecapital #techexplained #softwarebusiness #whyappsgetworse

title: The Real Reason Discord Left Cassandra
description:
Discord once took 20 seconds to load a single message, while the same app was serving billions of messages a day without breaking a sweat. That gap is the whole story.

In this video we walk through how Discord stored its messages, from their very first database to the system that now holds trillions of them. We start with MongoDB in 2015, see why it broke at 100 million messages, move to Cassandra and the clever partition-key and bucket design, then hit the wall that mattered: tombstones, garbage collector pauses, hot partitions, and a system the team was babysitting every weekend. Finally we look at the move to ScyllaDB, the Rust request-coalescing layer in front of it, and the migration of trillions of messages with zero downtime.

Along the way you pick up system design thinking that applies to almost any large app you will build: match your database to your access pattern, write the wishlist before you pick the tool, and put a smart layer in front to absorb the spikes.

title: The US Government Just Banned Anthropic’s New AI Model
description:
Anthropic's Fable 5 was not just another model bump. It was a real jump in long-running tasks, coding benchmarks, and the kind of intent-carrying behavior I care about most. Then the US government stepped in and access was abruptly cut off. So this is part hands-on review, part benchmark breakdown, and part "what exactly did we just watch happen?"

Fable 5 was released as a safer offshoot of Mythos 5, with a classifier in front of the more dangerous capability surface. I tested it before access disappeared, and the results were not subtle: stronger benchmark numbers, better long-task behavior, and a much better "pre-plan" style that made complex requests easier to steer. But the bigger story is the June 12 US government directive that forced Anthropic to disable access, including for foreign nationals and even some employees. If this standard becomes normal, it could change how every frontier model gets deployed.

title: How to Run a Trillion-Parameter AI at 1,000 Tokens a Second
description:
Xiaomi got a trillion-parameter model — one that trades blows with the frontier on coding benchmarks — to generate text at around 1,000 tokens a second. That's 10–20× faster than Claude's Opus or GPT, running on eight ordinary GPUs with no custom silicon. A model that big should be slow. This video is about the trick that makes it fast, and it isn't a smaller model or a better chip. It's the plumbing.

Running a model for one token isn't one operation — it's hundreds of tiny jobs handed to the GPU one after another, and every hand-off carries a few microseconds of pure setup. At 70 tokens a second that setup is noise. At 1,000, where your whole budget per token is under a millisecond, the setup alone blows it — "the launch overhead completely kills you." The fix is a megakernel: stop handing the GPU hundreds of jobs and hand it one. Fuse the entire model — every layer, every step — into a single kernel that starts once and runs end to end without ever stopping to switch. The catch is that it's brutal to build. Tear out those job boundaries and nothing keeps thousands of GPU workers in order, so it doesn't crash — it quietly returns a confidently wrong answer. You rebuild the ordering by hand, squeeze everything into kilobytes of fast memory, and re-implement every battle-tested library from scratch, fused to one exact model.

Xiaomi's real contribution is pulling all of that off at trillion-parameter scale, across eight chips, in production — folding even the chip-to-chip communication into the one nonstop kernel (a runtime they call TileRT). And it's becoming the main event: there's now research to generate megakernels automatically, and AI models that write GPU kernels better than the human experts they're put up against — the loop that makes AI faster is starting to be run by AI itself. For years the bottleneck was a smarter model. The model can sit perfectly still now and still get ten times faster by fixing the plumbing around it. (One honest caveat: the fast tier is gated, short-trial, costs more, and nobody outside has independently clocked that 1,000/s yet — hold the exact number loosely. The direction is clear.) If you'd watch a full deep-dive on AI writing its own kernels, say so in the comments — and subscribe for more under-the-hood breakdowns.

## Chapters
00:00 A trillion-parameter model at 1,000 words a second
00:25 Why speed is suddenly the bottleneck
00:54 The usual tricks — and the cost they all miss
01:41 The third cost nobody touches
01:56 Kernels: hundreds of tiny jobs per word
02:16 "At this speed, the launch overhead kills you"
02:52 A chef too fast to cook
03:29 One giant kernel — the megakernel
03:55 The goal: no bubbles
04:20 So why doesn't everyone do this?
04:45 It doesn't crash — it lies
05:55 Problem two: it won't fit
06:32 Problem three: no libraries left
07:12 Why Xiaomi's version is the hard one
07:43 TileRT and warp specialization
08:09 Megakernels become the main event
08:30 Now AI is writing the kernels
09:08 Fast enough to try many answers at once
09:31 Why the fast lane costs 3×
10:28 Software, not silicon — why it spreads
11:05 The slow part moved

- Xiaomi MiMo — TileRT and the 1,000 tok/s run — https://mimo.xiaomi.com/blog/mimo-tilert-1000tps
- Gizmochina on MiMo v2.5 Pro "UltraSpeed" mode — https://www.gizmochina.com/2026/06/09/xiaomi-mimo-v2-5-pro-ultraspeed-mode-1000-tokens-per-second/

title: 1,000+ Tokens/Sec: Google Just Shattered the AI Speed Limit (DiffusionGemma)
description:
In this video, we explore Google DeepMind's newly released DiffusionGemma model, a revolutionary paradigm shift that applies image-generation techniques to text by using uniform state diffusion to refine a canvas of pure noise over multiple bidirectional passes. This structural shift flips local LLMs from being memory-bound to compute-bound, theoretically unlocking generation speeds exceeding 1,000 tokens per second on an H100 GPU. To see how this architecture holds up in the real world, we deploy the model inside a RunPod container and benchmark it against practical engineering tasks prompting it to build a personal finance dashboard and a fully functional arcade game to see if its blazing speed is worth the quality tradeoff.

title: Recursive Self-Improvement Just Got Real (Anthropic + Recursive)
description:
What does it take to do research? Strip away the romance and you get a loop: propose → implement → run → validate → learn → choose. For the entire history of science, every station on that loop was staffed by humans.
This week, two organizations showed which stations no longer are.

Anthropic published "When AI builds itself," revealing that more than 80% of code merged into its codebase is now authored by Claude. Recursive published "First Steps Toward Automated AI Research," showing a system that walks the full loop on real AI infrastructure tasks – and chooses its own next experiment. And the Claude Fable 5 backlash showed us where the power goes once the loop automates: to whoever controls the evaluator.

*We explain:*
The research loop: the six stations of science, and which ones machines now occupy.
Anthropic's evidence
Recursive's results with recursive self-improving
The structural fact of the week and why that makes automated research arrive lopsided. The power station: why evaluators become the most contested objects in AI. 
The Fable backlash, reread

*Links mentioned:* 
Anthropic, "When AI builds itself":  https://www.anthropic.com/institute/recursive-self-improvement 
Recursive, "First Steps Toward Automated AI Research" https://www.recursive.com/articles/first-steps-toward-automated-ai-research
Initial reaction to Anthropic’s Fable limitations by Dr. Fei Fei Li: https://x.com/drfeifei/status/2064735920281313688 and Will Brown from Prime Intellect https://x.com/willccbb/status/2064524803885646291
Points to Anthropic for fast reaction: https://x.com/natolambert/status/2065064364772892750

Attention Span is here to show you AI isn't magic. Sometimes the most important shifts in the field come from the unglamorous middle of the research loop.

GitHub Copilot's new credit pricing ate my entire monthly allowance in one afternoon — so I put it head-to-head with Claude Code. Same model, same prompt, very different results.

In this Claude Code vs GitHub Copilot comparison, I give both tools an identical task — implementing a compensating action with refund logic (plus test cases) in a Dapr workflow — running Anthropic's latest model, Claude Fable 5, on max effort. I break down Copilot's June switch to credit-based pricing, why a single prompt consumed roughly 600 credits (about 9% of my monthly allowance), how much faster Claude Code completed the same work, and what my real token usage looks like on the $20 and $200 Claude Code plans. I also cover the developer experience I'll genuinely miss from Copilot (that diff view!), whether AI coding agent prices are sustainable, and how to think about value when every agentic coding tool runs the same frontier models.

title: Dana Lawson - Built for Humans. Now Agents Are Here. - AI Native DevCon June 2026
description:
Netlify was built around a human loop: a developer pushes code, scans the build logs, checks the deploy preview, decides it's good. Every part of the platform — the CLI, the API, the branch workflow — was designed assuming someone was paying attention.

Then agents started driving it. And things broke in unexpected ways.

Agents don't scan logs — they need structured, actionable signals or they retry blindly. Deploy previews designed for human eyeballs don't tell an agent whether the result is correct. CLI output written for readability becomes noise when a machine is parsing it for intent. The branch workflow that feels intuitive to a developer becomes an ambiguous decision tree for an agent with no intuition.

This talk is about what Netlify's platform revealed when agents came to work on it — and what Agent Experience (AX) means in practice: redesigning the surfaces, signals, and feedback loops that platforms expose so that humans and agents can build together without humans becoming the bottleneck.

Audiences will learn:
- What concretely breaks in build pipelines, CLI surfaces, and deploy workflows when agents start driving them
- What AX means in practice: actionable signals, machine-readable errors, and feedback loops designed for collaboration
- Why the scarce resource in an agent-assisted world isn't code, it's the judgment to build systems agents can actually work with

title: He Was Right... And Wrong | Inside Data Science Interview
description:
A complete data science mock interview covering exploratory data analysis (EDA), feature scaling, outlier detection, and regression modeling on the California Housing dataset. This session is a practical walkthrough of the real skills tested in data science and machine learning interviews - and why problem framing matters as much as technical execution.

Topics covered in this data science interview:
- Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) and interpreting `df.describe()` - count, mean, std, min, max
- Understanding median income as an encoded value, not raw currency
- Identifying a bimodal distribution and what it means for modeling
- Outlier detection using box plots and the IQR method (Q1, Q3, lower/upper fence)
- Feature scaling explained - StandardScaler vs MinMaxScaler and why scaling is needed
- Building a regression model with Random Forest Regressor
- The latitude prediction problem: why predicting latitude alone is a flawed target, and how to question whether a target even makes sense
- Technical round feedback - strengths, gaps, and next steps
- Product thinking for data scientists: Problem → User → Business Goal → Solution

This data science interview breakdown is useful for anyone preparing for machine learning interviews, practicing Python and scikit-learn, learning EDA and data preprocessing, or improving how they approach problem framing and model selection. Whether you're a beginner learning data science or an experienced candidate sharpening interview skills, this mock interview shows that being technically correct isn't the same as solving the right problem.

title: Pretrained LLMs Are Surrounded by Task Experts feat Yulu Gan from MIT
description:
today we are exploring the strange geometry of neural networks where diverse task experts are densely packed around pretrained weights with Yulu Gan from MIT CSAIL! 

his new paper "Neural Thickets" shows that simple random perturbation of the weights (RandOpt) can rival gradient-based post-training methods like GRPO. we'll walk through the paper step by step with a gentle introduction for beginners, dig into why these thickets emerge at all from first principles, and explore what this means for post-training, distillation and continual learning!

ps: check out the project blog over here (very visual): https://thickets.mit.edu/

title: Stop Cascading Failures  - Circuit Breaker & Bulkhead
description:
One slow dependency shouldn't take down your whole system. Circuit Breaker and Bulkhead patterns prevent cascading failures in distributed systems.

In this video, you'll see exactly how a single slow dependency consumes your entire thread pool, and how these two resilience patterns stop the cascade before it starts.

Circuit Breaker decides WHEN to stop calling a failing service. Bulkhead decides HOW MUCH of your system a failure can consume. Together, they turn total outages into graceful degradation.

title: 3 Takeaways After the US Government Bans Claude Fable 5
description:
A couple hours ago the US government had Anthropic pull Claude Fable 5 offline for everyone, just days after launch — here are my three takeaways.

Sign up for my FREE weekly newsletter, where I spill my unfiltered thoughts on the latest AI news, cool research, and projects I'm building: https://www.onchainaigarage.com/

🐦 Follow Tonbi on X for real-time AI x blockchain updates! https://x.com/tonbistudio

A quick response video on a genuinely unprecedented moment: a US government export-control directive led Anthropic to disable Claude Fable 5 for all customers worldwide, only days after it went live. I walk through what we know so far — the directive citing national security and foreign-national access, the fact that the "jailbreak" behind it was essentially asking the model to read a codebase and fix vulnerabilities (something Anthropic notes other models like GPT-5.5 can also do), and the irony that Dario Amodei had argued weeks earlier, in his "Policy on the AI Exponential" post, that the government should have exactly this power to block unsafe models. Then my three takeaways: (1) open source and decentralization have to win — two entities just switched off the most capable model on earth for everyone, which is the strongest case yet for local and decentralized AI (and the agent harnesses around the models, not just the models themselves); (2) the limits are showing — we got 3 days instead of the promised 2 weeks, pointing to real economic and capability ceilings and a worrying have/have-not divide; and (3) a more speculative thought — with an IPO reportedly ahead and heavy "this is dangerous" messaging around Mythos and Fable, the timing of a ban that hypes demand and saves compute is at least convenient. This is a developing story and the order may soften, so this is my read as of right now.

Timestamps:
0:00 - Anthropic pulls Fable 5 access
0:37 - The timeline + the export-control directive
1:21 - The "jailbreak" was just asking it to fix code
2:02 - The irony: Dario's own policy blog post
3:41 - Takeaway 1: open source & decentralization must win
5:28 - Takeaway 2: the economic and capability limits
6:29 - Takeaway 3: a convenient ban? (speculation)
7:54 - What happens next

What's your take — is this a real safety call, an economic one, or something else? And does it change how you think about local models? Let me know in the comments. This is a fast-moving story so I'll follow up if it develops — if you want that, please like and subscribe! 🦐✨

title: I Interviewed the Ex-Kimi & CapCut Product Lead Who Raised $40M to Build AI Agents
description:
I spent an incredible afternoon in Shenzhen with Leon (Xiaoming), ex-Product Lead at Kimi (Moonshot AI) and CapCut, diving deep into the future of AI-native collaboration.

Leon left ByteDance when Midjourney and ChatGPT blew up, joined Kimi during its darkest days, and witnessed its explosive growth. Now, he’s raised $40M+ to build YouWare (a vibe coding tool predating Lovable) and Bloome (an AI agent collaboration platform).

title: 🎥 Statistics 4.1 – Introduction to Probability | Sample Spaces, Events & Types of Probability
description:
Welcome to Chapter 4.1: Introduction to Probability! In this lesson, we begin the transition from descriptive statistics to the world of probability — the mathematical language of uncertainty. Before we can calculate probabilities, we need to understand outcomes, sample spaces, and the different ways probability can be defined.

We’ll cover:
✅ What a probability experiment is and how outcomes, sample spaces, and events are defined
— Coins, dice, raffle tickets, and more
— How to list outcomes systematically
✅ How to construct sample spaces using:
— Ordered pairs
— Patterns
— Tree diagrams for multi‑stage experiments
— Real examples like rolling two dice or modeling a family with three children
✅ The three types of probability:
— Subjective probability (educated guesses)
— Experimental probability (based on data and the Law of Large Numbers)
— Classical probability (theoretical, equally likely outcomes)
✅ How to calculate probabilities using each method
— Card draws, dice rolls, coin samples, and real‑world scenarios
— How to compute probabilities of combined events (e.g., “3 or 2,” “less than 4”)
✅ How to use Excel to compute classical probabilities with proper formatting
✅ Real‑world applications:
— Roadblock sampling
— Dessert choices at a dinner party
— Hitting a target multiple times
— Genetic probability using a Punnett square

You’ll see how probability connects to everyday decisions, scientific studies, and even biology. By the end of the video, you’ll be able to identify sample spaces, classify types of probability, and compute basic probabilities with confidence.

🎓 Perfect for:  
Introductory Statistics students
Business, social science, health, or STEM majors
Anyone learning how to quantify uncertainty and analyze chance events

Slide Deck/Excel Files Located here:

title: Coding an OS kernel in C and Assembly
description:
This is the first episode of my new series where I write my own operating system kernel. We'll start by writing our own bootloader, then adding support for putting text on the screen and reading the disk through BIOS interrupts in real mode.

We'll use this to load our kernel into memory and start executing it. The OS kernel itself will primarily be written in C, but some stuff needs to be written in Assembly, especially in the beginning. I'm going to show you how you can use regular gcc to produce 16 bit real mode compatible code.

Our next task is entering protected mode operation. We'll do this by creating a global descriptor table in order to setup segmented memory. Then we can enter protected mode and focus on getting the cpu interrupts to work. And then we'll just go from there.

Exciting project, no? Let's get to it.
JB

#OSdev

title: AIventure: Vibe Coding Journey
description:
Ian Ballantyne, Developer Relations Engineer at Google DeepMind, introduces AIventure, an open source retro dungeon crawler that doubles as a Gen AI masterclass for developers. Built on Angular and Phaser.js and powered by Gemma 4 open weights, the game teaches vibe coding and agentic behaviors through gameplay.

title: Qwen 3.5 MoE + TurboQuant + mem0: A Local RAG Chatbot That Remembers
description:
👉 Get started: https://mem0.ai/?via=julien. Promo code JULIEN: $19 off all payments within the first 3 months!

I upgraded my local RAG chatbot to a 35B Qwen mixture-of-experts at 32K context, running entirely on a MacBook. The trick is TheTom's TurboQuant fork of llama.cpp, which compresses the KV cache by ~60% with no measurable quality loss. This time it does real work: RAG over 94 full IEA energy reports, plus persistent cross-session memory with mem0: the bot remembers who I am after a full restart.



mem0 is an open-source memory layer for AI apps. It works with your existing stack, local or hosted: run it on your own machine while you build, plug into managed memory when you ship. 

The chatbot + mem0 integration & launch scripts: https://github.com/juliensimon/local-rag-chatbot/tree/feature/mem0

title: Build a Real-Time Chat Application on AWS
description:
🧪 Practice free in our AWS labs: https://kode.wiki/4org8ug

Ever wondered how a chat message reaches every online user instantly, without a single server running in the background? In this video we get absolute clarity on how real-time serverless systems actually work on AWS, and how five core services come together to make it happen.

We architect a production-ready chat application end to end: storing live connections and history in DynamoDB, absorbing traffic spikes with SQS, securing every interaction with IAM, processing events with Lambda, and keeping a persistent two-way channel open through API Gateway WebSockets. No jargon, just a clear mental model you'll keep for good.

🎯 By the end of this video you'll understand:
💠How DynamoDB acts as the shared memory layer for a serverless app
💠How SQS decouples fast broadcasts from slow database writes
💠How IAM roles grant your code exact permissions without hardcoded credentials
💠How Lambda wakes up, scales instantly, and disappears when the job is done
💠How WebSocket APIs deliver real-time messages without constant polling

👉 Don't just watch it, build it. Spin up your own real-time chat app in our free labs, from hand-writing IAM policies to watching messages go live over WebSockets: https://kode.wiki/4org8ug

⏰ Timestamps below 👇
00:00 - Why Real-Time Chat Apps Need a Different Architecture
01:08 - DynamoDB: The Shared Memory Layer
03:02 - Amazon SQS: The Shock Absorber for Traffic Spikes
04:51 - AWS IAM: The Security Badge for Your Code
06:53 - Hands-On Lab Checkpoint
07:12 - AWS Lambda: The Chat Handler & Chat Archiver
09:05 - API Gateway: Web Sockets & Real-Time Routing
11:20 - Conclusion + Capstone Project
